questions.text: Which of the following is not a main transition element?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Copper (Cu) is a main transition element commonly found in the d-block of the periodic table.
Gold (Au) is a main transition element commonly found in the d-block of the periodic table.
Silver (Ag) is a main transition element commonly found in the d-block of the periodic table.
Lead (Pb) is not a main transition element; it is a post-transition element found in the p-block of the periodic table.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D:Pb. Lead (Pb) is not considered a main transition element because it is part of the p-block of the periodic table, not the d-block where main transition elements are typically found. Options A, B, and C are all main transition elements commonly found in the d-block.

questions.text: Which of the following transition series is of main transition series?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The first transition series refers to the transition elements filling the 3d orbital. This is not the main transition series.
The 2nd transition series corresponds to elements in the 4d orbital. This is not the main transition series.
The 3rd transition series consists of elements in the 5d orbital. This is not the main transition series.
The main transition series includes all transition elements in the d and f orbitals, encompassing the first, second, and third transition series.

questions.explanation: The main transition series includes all transition elements in the d and f orbitals, so the correct answer is 'All of these'. The first, second, and third transition series refer to specific sets of transition elements in the 3d, 4d, and 5d orbitals, respectively, but they do not encompass all transition elements.

questions.text: Second transition series starts from Yttrium (Y39) and ends at:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ag is part of the second transition series, but it is not the last element. It is a precious metal known for its conductivity.
Zr is indeed part of the second transition series, but it comes early in the series, right after Yttrium.
Rh is also part of the second transition series, known for its excellent corrosion resistance, but it is not the last element.
Cd is the correct answer as it is the last element of the second transition series, following Silver (Ag).

questions.explanation: The second transition series consists of elements from Yttrium (Y) to Cadmium (Cd) in the periodic table. The series starts with Yttrium (Y) and ends with Cadmium (Cd). Option D is correct because Cd is the last element in this series. Options A, B, and C list elements that are part of the second transition series but not the last one. Therefore, they are incorrect as the answer to this question.

questions.text: Which of the following is incomplete transition series?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The first transition series is complete, so this is not the correct answer.
The second transition series is complete, so this is not the correct answer.
The third transition series is complete, so this is not the correct answer.
The fourth transition series is incomplete with elements like Rf, Db, Sg, Bh, Hs, Mt, Ds, and Rg. Hence, this is the correct answer.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the Fourth transition series because it is incomplete compared to the other options. The first three transition series (1st, 2nd, and 3rd) are complete, while the fourth series is still ongoing with elements beyond Lawrencium. Therefore, options A, B, and C are incorrect as they refer to complete transition series.

questions.text: Which of the following orbitals are under the process of completion in third transition series?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This orbital belongs to the third transition series, but it is not under completion at this stage.
This orbital belongs to the fourth transition series, not the third one where completion is occurring.
Correct! The 5d orbitals are under completion in the third transition series.
This orbital belongs to the sixth transition series, not the third one where completion is happening.

questions.explanation: In the third transition series, the 5d orbitals are in the process of completion. This is why option C,5d, is the correct answer. Options A, B, and D are incorrect as they refer to orbitals from different transition series that are not currently under completion.

questions.text: The electronic configuration of d- Block elements can be represented as;
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option incorrectly suggests that the d orbital is one energy level higher than the s orbital, which is not the case for d-Block elements.
This option correctly represents the electronic configuration of d-Block elements where the d orbital is one energy level lower than the s orbital.
This option does not account for the specific relationship between the energy levels of the d and s orbitals in d-Block elements.
This option incorrectly suggests that the s orbital is one energy level higher than the d orbital, which is not the case for d-Block elements.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: (n-1) d, ns. In the electronic configuration of d-Block elements, the d orbital is filled with electrons one energy level lower than the s orbital. This arrangement allows for the proper filling of electrons in the d-Block elements. The other options are incorrect because they do not accurately represent the energy levels of the d and s orbitals in d-Block elements.

questions.text: 4f series starts from atomic number;
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Correct! The 4f series starts from atomic number 57, which corresponds to the element Lanthanum.
Incorrect. This atomic number does not correspond to the start of the 4f series.
Incorrect. This atomic number is not where the 4f series begins.
Incorrect. This atomic number is close, but not where the 4f series actually starts.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A:57. The 4f series begins at atomic number 57, which corresponds to Lanthanum. Options B, C, and D do not reflect the correct starting point of the 4f series.

questions.text: 5f series of transition elements starts from atomic number?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not correct. The 5f series of transition elements starts at atomic number 89, not 75.
This is not correct. The 5f series of transition elements starts at atomic number 89, not 81.
This is not correct. The 5f series of transition elements starts at atomic number 89, not 85.
Correct! The 5f series of transition elements starts at atomic number 89.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is option D,89. The 5f series of transition elements begins at atomic number 89. Options A, B, and C are incorrect because they do not correspond to the correct starting atomic number of the 5f series.

questions.text: Copper shows slightly increase in atomic radius in the 3d series, this is due to;
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Increasing shell number typically leads to larger atomic radii, but this is not the primary reason for the slight increase observed in the atomic radius of copper.
While an increase in nuclear charge generally leads to a decrease in atomic radius due to greater attraction of electrons, it doesn't explain the slight increase seen in copper.
The correct answer. In copper, repulsion among outer electrons due to electron-electron interactions can lead to a slight increase in atomic radius compared to other elements in the series.
Although shielding effect can affect atomic size, the d-electrons in transition metals do not shield as effectively, so this is not the reason for the slight increase in copper's atomic radius.

questions.explanation: In the 3d series of transition metals, copper shows a slight increase in atomic radius primarily due to electron-electron repulsions among the outer electrons. These repulsions slightly counterbalance the increase in nuclear charge, which usually causes a decrease in atomic radius. Although an increase in nuclear charge and a greater shielding effect are factors that influence atomic size, they do not account for the observed increase in copper's atomic radius. The increase in shell number is also not applicable as the primary reason in this context.

questions.text: The greater densities of transition metals is due to
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option does not directly explain the greater densities of transition metals. The density is more related to the mass and volume of the atoms.
While shielding effect can influence properties of transition metals, it is not the primary reason for their greater densities. Think more about the mass to volume ratio.
Correct! The greater densities of transition metals are due to their higher mass to volume ratio. This means more mass is packed into a smaller volume, resulting in higher density.
Size alone does not determine density. It is the mass to volume ratio that plays a key role in determining the density of transition metals.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: Greater mass to volume ratio. Transition metals have higher densities compared to other elements due to their greater mass to volume ratio. This means that a larger amount of mass is packed into a smaller volume, leading to higher densities. The other options do not directly address the relationship between mass and volume, which is crucial in understanding the density of transition metals.

questions.text: The most common oxidation states of the “d” block elements are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The most common oxidation states of d-block elements are not +3 and +5.
This option is incorrect. While +2 is a common oxidation state, +5 is not typically observed for d-block elements.
Correct! The most common oxidation states of d-block elements are indeed +2 and +3.
This option is incorrect. While +3 is common, +4 is not typically a common oxidation state for d-block elements.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is +2, +3. The d-block elements typically exhibit oxidation states of +2 and +3 due to the filling of the d orbitals. Option A is incorrect as +5 is not a common oxidation state for d-block elements. Option B is incorrect as +5 is not typically observed. Option D is incorrect as +4 is not a common oxidation state for d-block elements.

questions.text: Which one of the following catalyst is not used in the hydrogenation of alkenes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Platinum (Pt) is commonly used as a catalyst in the hydrogenation of alkenes.
Nickel (Ni) is commonly used as a catalyst in the hydrogenation of alkenes.
Palladium (Pd) is commonly used as a catalyst in the hydrogenation of alkenes.
Iron (Fe) is not typically used as a catalyst in the hydrogenation of alkenes. It is more commonly used in other types of reactions.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D:Fe. Iron (Fe) is not commonly used as a catalyst in the hydrogenation of alkenes, unlike platinum, nickel, and palladium which are frequently utilized in this reaction. Platinum, nickel, and palladium have been found to be more effective catalysts for this specific process due to their reactivity and efficiency. Therefore, Fe stands out as the catalyst that is not typically used in the hydrogenation of alkenes.

questions.text: The catalyst used in the Haber Bosch process of Ammonia syntheses is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not the correct catalyst for the Haber Bosch process of Ammonia syntheses.
Correct! Fe2O3 is the catalyst used in the Haber Bosch process of Ammonia syntheses.
This is not the correct catalyst for the Haber Bosch process of Ammonia syntheses.
This is not the correct catalyst for the Haber Bosch process of Ammonia syntheses.

questions.explanation: In the Haber Bosch process of Ammonia syntheses, the catalyst used is Fe2O3. This catalyst helps facilitate the conversion of nitrogen and hydrogen into ammonia efficiently. The other options, V2O5, PbO, and Cr2O3, are not the correct catalysts for this specific process.

questions.text: Which of the following pair of ions shows diamagnetic properties?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sn2+ has a completely filled d orbital (4d10) with all paired electrons, making it diamagnetic. Zn2+ also has a completely filled d orbital (3d10) and is diamagnetic as well.
Mn2+ has unpaired electrons in its d orbital (3d5), making it paramagnetic. Zn2+ has a completely filled d orbital (3d10) and is diamagnetic.
Fe2+ has unpaired electrons in its d orbital (3d6), making it paramagnetic. Mn2+ has unpaired electrons in its d orbital (3d5) and is also paramagnetic.
Cu+ has a completely filled d orbital (3d10) and is diamagnetic. Cr3+ has unpaired electrons in its d orbital (3d3), making it paramagnetic.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: Sn2+ & Zn2+. Both ions have completely filled d orbitals with all paired electrons, leading to diamagnetic properties. Option B, C, and D have at least one ion with unpaired electrons in the d orbital, making them paramagnetic and not exhibiting diamagnetic properties.

questions.text: Among the following which one show more paramagnetic nature?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Vanadium in +2 oxidation state has unpaired electrons, making it paramagnetic.
Chromium in +3 oxidation state does not have unpaired electrons, so it is not paramagnetic.
Manganese in +2 oxidation state has unpaired electrons, making it paramagnetic.
Nickel in +2 oxidation state does not have unpaired electrons, so it is not paramagnetic.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: Mn2+. Manganese in the +2 oxidation state has five unpaired electrons, making it highly paramagnetic. The other options do not have unpaired electrons, so they are not paramagnetic.

questions.text: Which of the following shows least paramagnetic properties?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Vanadium (V) in the +3 oxidation state has 2 unpaired electrons in its d orbitals, making it exhibit paramagnetic properties.
Chromium (Cr) in the +3 oxidation state has 3 unpaired electrons in its d orbitals, making it paramagnetic.
Manganese (Mn) in the +3 oxidation state has 4 unpaired electrons in its d orbitals, making it paramagnetic.
Iron (Fe) in the +3 oxidation state has 5 unpaired electrons in its d orbitals, making it the species with the least paramagnetic properties among the options given.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: Fe3+. Iron (Fe) in the +3 oxidation state has the least paramagnetic properties among the options provided because it has the highest number of unpaired electrons (5) in its d orbitals. This results in strong paramagnetic behavior. The other options have lower numbers of unpaired electrons, leading to stronger paramagnetic properties.

questions.text: Which of the following shows the increasing order of paramagnetic properties?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect as it does not follow the correct increasing order of paramagnetic properties.
This option is correct as it shows the increasing order of paramagnetic properties based on the number of unpaired electrons in the d orbitals of transition metals.
This option is incorrect as it does not follow the correct increasing order of paramagnetic properties.
This option is incorrect as it does not show the correct increasing order of paramagnetic properties.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: Ti3+<V3+<Cr3+<Mn2+<Fe3+. This is the correct increasing order of paramagnetic properties based on the number of unpaired electrons in the d orbitals of these transition metals. Option A, C, and D do not follow the correct order and should be eliminated.

questions.text: Alloy formation by d-block elements is due to;
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Because all d-block elements are metals
options.explanation: This option is incorrect as not all d-block elements are metals. The ability to form alloys is not solely dependent on being a metal.
While variable oxidation states are a characteristic of d-block elements, it is not the primary reason for alloy formation. This option is incorrect.

options.text: They have about the same atomic size
options.explanation: Correct! The similar atomic size of d-block elements facilitates alloy formation by allowing for effective mixing and bonding.

options.text: They form metallic bond with each other
options.explanation: While d-block elements can form metallic bonds, the key factor for alloy formation is their similar atomic size. This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Alloy formation by d-block elements is primarily due to their similar atomic sizes, which enable them to mix effectively and form stable alloys. This similarity in atomic size allows for the creation of solid solutions with enhanced properties. Options A, B, and D are incorrect as alloy formation is not solely based on being metals, showing variable oxidations, or forming metallic bonds. The correct answer is option C.

questions.text: Which of the following have Blue color?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Scandium (Sc) in its +3 oxidation state does not typically exhibit a blue color. This option is not correct.
Iron (Fe) in its +3 oxidation state does not produce a blue color. This option is not correct.
Copper (Cu) in its +2 oxidation state is known to have a characteristic blue color. This is the correct option.
Manganese (Mn) in its +2 oxidation state does not result in a blue color. This option is not correct.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C:Cu2+. Copper ions in their +2 oxidation state exhibit a blue color due to the d-d transition. This transition involves the movement of electrons within the d orbitals, leading to the absorption of certain wavelengths of light and the reflection of blue light. The other options do not correspond to metal ions known for their blue coloration.

questions.text: Which of the following ion do not have blue color
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chromium(III) ions have a characteristic blue color due to d-d transitions.
Cobalt(II) ions are often pink in color due to their electronic configuration.
Copper(II) ions have a blue color in solution due to d-d transitions.
Nickel(II) ions do not exhibit a blue color as they lack the necessary electronic configuration for d-d transitions to cause color.

questions.explanation: Nickel(II) ions do not have a blue color because they have a fully filled d orbital with no unpaired electrons, which is required for d-d transitions to occur and produce color. The other options, such as Cr(III), Co(II), and Cu(II), have unpaired electrons in their d orbitals, leading to characteristic colors like blue and pink.

questions.text: Which of the following ion is colorless?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Scandium in the +3 oxidation state has an empty 3d orbital and is colorless due to the absence of unpaired electrons.
Titanium in the +4 oxidation state has a filled 3d orbital and is colorless due to the absence of unpaired electrons.
Zinc in the +2 oxidation state has a completely filled 3d orbital and is colorless due to the absence of unpaired electrons.
All the given ions (Scandium +3, Titanium +4, Zinc +2) are colorless due to the absence of unpaired electrons in their d orbitals, making this option correct.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is option D:All of the above.All the ions mentioned (Scandium +3, Titanium +4, Zinc +2) are colorless because they do not have any unpaired electrons in their d orbitals. This absence of unpaired electrons results in the ions being colorless. Options A, B, and C are incorrect because they each have specific electronic configurations that lead to their colorless nature.

questions.text: Platinum is replaced by V2O5 as a catalyst in the contact process because platinum is susceptible to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Platinum is not significantly affected by oxygen in this context, thus it remains stable and active even in the presence of oxygen. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
Moisture does not generally cause deactivation of platinum in the contact process, making this option incorrect.
While platinum can be affected by sulphur compounds, in the context of the contact process, arsenic is the more significant poison. Thus, this option is incorrect.
Platinum is indeed susceptible to poisoning by arsenic, which can significantly reduce its catalytic efficiency, making this the correct option.

questions.explanation: Platinum is replaced by V2O5 in the contact process primarily because it is vulnerable to poisoning by arsenic. Arsenic can accumulate on the platinum surface, blocking its active sites and thus diminishing its catalytic effectiveness. The other options, such as poisoning by oxygen, moisture, or sulphur, do not have the same detrimental effect on platinum's performance in this specific application.

questions.text: If the electronic configuration of transition metal ion (M^3+) is {Ar} 3d^4, atomic number of metal is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The electronic configuration of {Ar} 3d4 suggests that the metal ion M3+ originally had more electrons, corresponding to a higher atomic number than 22.
This option is incorrect. An atomic number of 23 corresponds to the configuration {Ar} 3d3 for a M3+ ion, not 3d4.
This option is incorrect. The electronic configuration for a M3+ ion with atomic number 24 would be {Ar} 3d3, not 3d4.
This option is correct. The metal with atomic number 25 is manganese (Mn). The Mn3+ ion removes three electrons from the neutral atom's configuration {Ar} 3d5 4s2, resulting in {Ar} 3d4.

questions.explanation: The electronic configuration {Ar} 3d4 for the M3+ ion indicates that three electrons have been removed from the neutral atom. The neutral atom's configuration would be {Ar} 3d5 4s2, which corresponds to the element manganese (Mn) with an atomic number of 25. Therefore, the correct answer is Option D. Options A, B, and C are incorrect as they do not match the electronic configuration of the M3+ ion derived from atomic numbers 22, 23, or 24.

questions.text: 20 to 25% of 18 carat gold is composed of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Iron (Fe) is not commonly used in the composition of 18 carat gold.
Zinc (Zn) is not commonly used in the composition of 18 carat gold.
Copper (Cu) is commonly used in the composition of 18 carat gold.
Manganese (Mn) is not commonly used in the composition of 18 carat gold.

questions.explanation: In 18 carat gold, 75% is pure gold while the remaining 25% is made up of other metals. Copper (Cu) is commonly used to make up this portion of the composition, making Option C the correct answer. The other options are incorrect as they do not align with the typical composition of 18 carat gold.

questions.text: Which metal has the lowest m.p?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Copper (Cu) is not the correct answer. While copper has a relatively high melting point compared to many other metals, there is another metal in the d and f block elements with a lower melting point.
Gold (Au) is not the correct answer. Gold has a high melting point due to its strong metallic bonds, making it one of the elements with a higher melting point in the d and f block.
Mercury (Hg) is the correct answer. Mercury is a unique metal that is a liquid at room temperature, indicating it has the lowest melting point among the d and f block elements.
Manganese (Mn) is not the correct answer. While manganese has a relatively high melting point compared to some other metals, it is not the metal with the lowest melting point in the d and f block.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Hg (Mercury). Mercury is a unique metal that is a liquid at room temperature, indicating it has the lowest melting point among the d and f block elements. Copper (Cu), Gold (Au), and Manganese (Mn) all have higher melting points compared to Mercury, making them incorrect choices for this question.

questions.text: Rutile is an ore of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Rutile is not an ore of Manganese.
Rutile is not an ore of Chromium.
Correct! Rutile is an ore of Titanium.
Incorrect. Rutile is indeed an ore of Titanium.

questions.explanation: Rutile is a mineral composed primarily of Titanium dioxide, hence it is an ore of Titanium. Manganese and Chromium are not related to rutile as ores. The option 'None of these' is incorrect as rutile is specifically an ore of Titanium.

questions.text: Stainless steel is an alloy of iron which contains:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Stainless steel does not typically contain Magnesium (Mg) or Aluminum (Al).
Stainless steel commonly contains Chromium (Cr) for corrosion resistance, but not Magnesium (Mg).
Stainless steel often contains Chromium (Cr) and Nickel (Ni) for enhanced strength and corrosion resistance, making this the correct option.
Copper (Cu) and Zinc (Zn) are not typically found in stainless steel alloys.

questions.explanation: Stainless steel is an alloy that typically contains Chromium (Cr) and Nickel (Ni) among other elements. Chromium provides corrosion resistance, while Nickel enhances strength. This is why option C, Cr and Ni, is the correct answer. The other options do not align with the composition of stainless steel.

questions.text: Which of the following ions has the smallest radii?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nickel (Ni) with a 2+ charge has the smallest radii due to its higher effective nuclear charge and less electron shielding.
Vanadium (V) with a 2+ charge has larger radii compared to Ni due to its electron configuration differences.
Titanium (Ti) with a 2+ charge has larger radii compared to Ni due to its electron configuration differences.
Manganese (Mn) with a 2+ charge has larger radii compared to Ni due to its electron configuration differences.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Ni2+. Ni2+ has the smallest radii among the given options due to its higher effective nuclear charge and less electron shielding, leading to a stronger attraction to the nucleus. Vanadium, Titanium, and Manganese have larger radii because of differences in their electron configurations, resulting in weaker attraction to the nucleus compared to Nickel.

questions.text: The transition metal forms complexes because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the formation of complexes is not directly related to the size of transition metals.

options.text: They have large effective nuclear charge
options.explanation: Correct! Transition metals form complexes because of their large effective nuclear charge, which influences their ability to bond with ligands.
This is not the primary reason transition metals form complexes, making this option incorrect.
This option is incorrect as transition metals do have specific characteristics that lead to the formation of complexes.

questions.explanation: Transition metals have large effective nuclear charges, meaning they have a strong attraction for electrons. This property allows them to form complexes by bonding with ligands, which are molecules or ions that donate pairs of electrons to the metal ion. The other options are incorrect because the size, density, or lack of specific property do not directly relate to the formation of complexes by transition metals.

questions.text: The absorption of hydrogen by palladium is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option refers to a different chemical process and is not related to the absorption of hydrogen by palladium.
While this option involves hydrogen, it specifically relates to the addition of hydrogen to a compound, not its absorption by palladium.
Correct! Occlusion is the term used to describe the absorption of hydrogen by palladium.
This option is not correct as the process of hydrogen absorption by palladium has a specific term associated with it.

questions.explanation: The absorption of hydrogen by palladium is called occlusion. This term specifically refers to the process where hydrogen is absorbed into the palladium structure. Reduction is the process of gaining electrons, not related to hydrogen absorption. Hydrogenation is the addition of hydrogen to a compound. 'None of these' is incorrect as there is a specific term, occlusion, for the absorption of hydrogen by palladium.

questions.text: Platinum metal can be dissolved in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Platinum does not dissolve in hot and concentrated HCl alone.
Platinum does not dissolve in hot and concentrated HNO3 alone.
Platinum does not dissolve in hot and concentrated H2SO4 alone.
Platinum can be dissolved in a mixture of concentrated HCl and HNO3 due to the unique properties of these acids when combined.

questions.explanation: Platinum metal can be dissolved in a mixture of concentrated HCl and HNO3 because this combination provides the necessary conditions for the dissolution process. The other options of using individual acids alone do not facilitate the dissolution of platinum due to their specific chemical properties when isolated.

questions.text: Which element shows lowest melting point?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Zinc (Zn) shows the lowest melting point among the given elements. This is because Zn has a relatively low atomic size and weak metallic bonding, leading to a lower melting point compared to the other options.
Scandium (Sc) has a higher melting point compared to Zinc. Scandium is a transition metal with stronger metallic bonding, resulting in a higher melting point.
Chromium (Cr) has a higher melting point compared to Zinc. Chromium's atomic structure and bonding characteristics contribute to a higher melting point.
Cobalt (Co) has a higher melting point compared to Zinc. Cobalt's atomic properties and bonding interactions result in a higher melting point.

questions.explanation: Zinc (Zn) shows the lowest melting point among the given elements due to its atomic size and weak metallic bonding. Scandium (Sc), Chromium (Cr), and Cobalt (Co) have higher melting points compared to Zinc because of their atomic structures and bonding characteristics.

questions.text: Which of the following group's elements show maximum binding energy?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Elements in the IIIB group do not show maximum binding energy compared to other options in the question.
Elements in the IVB group do not show maximum binding energy compared to other options in the question.
Elements in the IIB group do not show maximum binding energy compared to other options in the question.
Elements in the VB group show maximum binding energy among the given options.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: VB. In the d and f block elements, the elements in the VB group exhibit the maximum binding energy compared to the other groups mentioned in the question. Elements in the IIIB, IVB, and IIB groups do not show the highest binding energy as the elements in the VB group do.

questions.text: _ are non stoichiometric compound.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Interstitial compounds are non-stoichiometric compounds where small atoms or molecules occupy the interstitial spaces in the crystal lattice of a host metal, leading to a variable composition.
Coordination compounds are formed by coordination bonds between a central metal ion and ligands, but they are typically not non-stoichiometric in nature.
Ionic compounds are typically stoichiometric, meaning they have a fixed ratio of elements in their chemical formula.
Covalent compounds are formed by sharing of electrons between atoms and are usually stoichiometric in nature.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Interstitial compound' because non-stoichiometric compounds like interstitial compounds have variable compositions due to the presence of small atoms or molecules in interstitial spaces. Coordination compounds, ionic compounds, and covalent compounds are typically stoichiometric and do not exhibit the same variability in composition as non-stoichiometric compounds.

questions.text: Which element if present as impurity in the ore is evolved as a gas?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Silicon is not typically evolved as a gas when present as an impurity in the ore.
Manganese is not typically evolved as a gas when present as an impurity in the ore.
Sulphur is the correct answer as it can be evolved as a gas when present as an impurity in the ore.
Phosphorus is not typically evolved as a gas when present as an impurity in the ore.

questions.explanation: When impurities like Sulphur are present in ores, they can be evolved as gases during the extraction process. Sulphur, specifically, is known to form gaseous compounds under certain conditions. Silicon, Manganese, and Phosphorus do not exhibit the same gas-evolving properties as Sulphur in this context. Therefore, the correct answer is Sulphur.

questions.text: Oxidation state _ is exhibited by nearly all outer transition elements.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct answer as nearly all outer transition elements exhibit an oxidation state of +3.
This is incorrect as outer transition elements typically do not exhibit a -3 oxidation state.
This is incorrect as outer transition elements typically exhibit a higher oxidation state than +2.
This is incorrect as outer transition elements typically exhibit a positive oxidation state.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is +3 because nearly all outer transition elements exhibit this oxidation state. Outer transition elements are known for their variable oxidation states, but the most common one among them is +3. The other options are incorrect because outer transition elements do not typically exhibit -3, +2, or -2 oxidation states.

questions.text: Interstitial compounds are not formed by the combination of transition metals and:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hydrogen can form interstitial compounds with transition metals, so this is not the correct answer.
Fluorine does not form interstitial compounds with transition metals, making it the correct answer.
Nitrogen can form interstitial compounds with transition metals, so this is not the correct answer.
Boron can form interstitial compounds with transition metals, so this is not the correct answer.

questions.explanation: Interstitial compounds are formed when small atoms like hydrogen, boron, or carbon occupy the interstitial sites in the crystal lattice of transition metals. Fluorine, on the other hand, is too large to fit into these interstitial sites, making it unable to form interstitial compounds with transition metals. Therefore, option B (F) is the correct answer. Options A, C, and D are incorrect as hydrogen, nitrogen, and boron can all form interstitial compounds with transition metals.

questions.text: Inner transition elements are also called?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lanthanides are a series of inner transition elements.
Actinides are another series of inner transition elements.
Rare earth metals are also part of the inner transition elements.
This is the correct answer because lanthanides, actinides, and rare earth metals are all types of inner transition elements.

questions.text: Which of the following pair of ions show ferromagnetic properties?

questions.text: Which one of the following is the most stable oxidations state of chromium?

Correct! Cr+3 is the most stable oxidation state of chromium.
This is not the most stable oxidation state of chromium.
This is not the most stable oxidation state of chromium.

questions.explanation: The most stable oxidation state of chromium is +3 because it follows the 18-electron rule. This rule states that the most stable oxidation states of transition metals are those that result in a full or half-full d subshell. In the case of chromium, the +3 oxidation state results in a half-full d subshell, making it more stable than +2, +4, or +6. Therefore, option B is correct, while options A, C, and D are incorrect.

questions.text: Which of the following metals has seven electrons in its 3d subshell in the +2 oxidation state?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In the +2 oxidation state, Cr24 loses two electrons from its 4s subshell, resulting in the configuration [Ar] 3d4. Therefore, it has 4 electrons in the 3d subshell, not 7. Hence, this option is incorrect.

In the +2 oxidation state, Fe26 loses two electrons from its 4s subshell, resulting in the configuration [Ar] 3d6. Therefore, it has 6 electrons in the 3d subshell, not 7. Hence, this option is incorrect.
In the +2 oxidation state, Co27 loses two electrons from its 4s subshell, resulting in the configuration [Ar] 3d7. It correctly has 7 electrons in the 3d subshell. Hence, this option is correct.

In the +2 oxidation state, Cu29 loses one electron from its 4s subshell and one from its 3d subshell, resulting in the configuration [Ar] 3d9. Therefore, it has 9 electrons in the 3d subshell, not 7. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The electronic configuration of the cobalt atom (Co) is [Ar] 4s2 3d7. When it loses two electrons to form Co2+, the electrons are removed from the 4s subshell, resulting in the configuration [Ar] 3d7. Thus, Co2+ has exactly 7 electrons in its 3d subshell, making it the correct answer. Conversely, Cr2+ has 4 electrons in its 3d subshell, Fe2+ has 6, and Cu2+ has 9, which do not match the requirement of having 7 electrons in the 3d subshell.

questions.text: Finely divided iron combines with CO to give
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Iron typically forms pentacarbonyl complexes, such as Fe(CO)5, when finely divided iron reacts with carbon monoxide.
Fe2(CO)9 is not typically formed by simply combining finely divided iron with CO. It is a dimeric iron carbonyl.
Fe3(CO)12 is a trimeric iron carbonyl, not formed directly from the reaction of iron and CO under ordinary conditions.
Fe(CO)6 does not exist as a stable compound because it would imply a coordination number of six for iron, which is not common in iron carbonyls.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Fe(CO)5. When finely divided iron reacts with carbon monoxide, it forms iron pentacarbonyl, or Fe(CO)5. This is a well-known volatile compound formed under these conditions. Other options such as Fe2(CO)9 and Fe3(CO)12 are also iron carbonyls but are not formed directly from the reaction of iron with CO. Fe(CO)6 is not a stable or known compound, as iron typically does not form six-coordinate carbonyl complexes.

questions.text: Which of the following is not correct?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Rusting of iron can be stopped by increasing the concentration of CO2 in water
options.explanation: Increasing CO2 concentration in water can lead to the formation of carbonic acid, which may accelerate rusting rather than prevent it.

options.text: Rusting of iron is electrochemical in nature
options.explanation: Rusting involves electrochemical reactions where iron acts as an anode and oxygen acts as a cathode, leading to oxidation.

options.text: Rusting of iron takes place in moist air
options.explanation: Moist air provides the necessary water and oxygen for the electrochemical process of rusting to occur.

options.text: Rusting of iron produces hydrated iron (III) oxide
options.explanation: Rust is primarily composed of hydrated iron (III) oxide, which is the result of the oxidation of iron.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A. Rusting of iron is an electrochemical process that requires the presence of water and oxygen. Increasing the concentration of CO2 in water may lead to the formation of carbonic acid, which can lower the pH and potentially accelerate rusting. Option B is correct because rusting involves electrochemical reactions. Option C is correct as moist air provides the conditions necessary for rusting. Option D is correct because the end product of rusting is primarily hydrated iron (III) oxide.

questions.text: Which of the following alloys contains(s) Cu and Zn?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Bronze is primarily composed of copper (Cu) and tin (Sn), not zinc (Zn).
Brass is an alloy that consists of copper (Cu) and zinc (Zn), making it the correct answer.
Gun-metal typically consists of copper (Cu), tin (Sn), and zinc (Zn), but it is primarily recognized for its tin content.
Type metal is generally made from lead (Pb), antimony (Sb), and tin (Sn), and does not contain copper (Cu) or zinc (Zn).

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Brass, which is an alloy made from copper (Cu) and zinc (Zn). While both bronze and gun-metal contain copper, bronze is primarily a copper-tin alloy, and gun-metal is known for its copper-tin-zinc composition, with emphasis on tin. Type metal does not contain copper or zinc as it is composed of lead, antimony, and tin.

questions.text: The rusting of iron is catalysed by which of the following?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Iron itself is the metal that undergoes rusting and does not act as a catalyst in the process.
Oxygen is essential for the rusting process but acts as a reactant, not a catalyst.
Zinc is used to prevent rusting in a process called galvanization; it does not catalyze rusting.
The presence of H+ ions, often from acid rain, accelerates the rusting process by increasing the conductivity of the water film on the iron surface.

questions.explanation: The rusting of iron is an electrochemical process that involves the oxidation of iron in the presence of moisture and oxygen. H+ ions, commonly found in acidic conditions, can accelerate this process by increasing the electrical conductivity of the aqueous layer on the iron, thereby facilitating the transfer of electrons. This makes H+ ions a catalyst in the rusting process. In contrast, Fe is the material that rusts, O2 is a necessary reactant but not a catalyst, and Zn is used to protect iron from rusting, not to catalyze it.

questions.text: Which of the following is soluble in water?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Silver fluoride (AgF) is soluble in water, unlike most other silver halides.
Silver chloride (AgCl) is not soluble in water; it forms a white precipitate.
Silver bromide (AgBr) is not soluble in water; it forms a pale yellow precipitate.
Silver iodide (AgI) is not soluble in water; it forms a yellow precipitate.

questions.explanation: Among the silver halides, AgF is unique because it is soluble in water. This is due to the relatively small size of the fluoride ion, which allows it to interact more effectively with water molecules. In contrast, AgCl, AgBr, and AgI are not soluble in water and form precipitates due to their larger halide ions, which do not dissolve well in water.

questions.text: Gold dissolves in aqua regia forming
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: AuCl is not typically formed in the reaction of gold with aqua regia.
Au(NO3)3 is gold(III) nitrate, which is not formed in this reaction.
AuCl3 is gold(III) chloride, a possible intermediate but not the final product in aqua regia.
HAuCl4 is chloroauric acid, the product of gold reacting with aqua regia.

questions.explanation: When gold (Au) dissolves in aqua regia, a mixture of concentrated hydrochloric acid and nitric acid, it forms chloroauric acid (HAuCl4). Aqua regia is capable of dissolving gold because the nitric acid oxidizes gold to gold ions, which are then stabilized by the chloride ions from hydrochloric acid to form the complex HAuCl4. The other options, AuCl and Au(NO3)3, are not products of this reaction. AuCl3 may form transiently, but it is not the final stable compound.

questions.text: Zinc oxide is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Basic oxides react with acids to form salt and water. Zinc oxide, however, also reacts with bases, which is not a characteristic of basic oxides.
Amphoteric oxides can react with both acids and bases. Zinc oxide exhibits this behavior, reacting with hydrochloric acid to form zinc chloride and with sodium hydroxide to form sodium zincate.
Acidic oxides react with bases to form salt and water. Zinc oxide does not exclusively react with bases, indicating it is not purely acidic.
Neutral oxides do not react with either acids or bases. Zinc oxide does react with both, ruling out this option.

questions.explanation: Zinc oxide is classified as an amphoteric oxide because it reacts with both acids and bases. When zinc oxide (ZnO) reacts with hydrochloric acid (HCl), it forms zinc chloride (ZnCl2) and water, demonstrating its acidic behavior. Conversely, when zinc oxide reacts with sodium hydroxide (NaOH), it forms sodium zincate (Na2ZnO2), showing its basic nature.The incorrect options are as follows: Option A (Basic oxide) is incorrect because zinc oxide does not exclusively react with acids. Option C (Acidic oxide) is incorrect because zinc oxide does not exclusively react with bases. Option D (Neutral oxide) is incorrect as neutral oxides do not react with either acids or bases, unlike zinc oxide.

questions.text: Variable oxidation states is shown by
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Normal elements are those that typically have a fixed oxidation state and do not exhibit variability in their chemistry as seen in transition metals.
While metallic elements can have multiple oxidation states, not all do. It is primarily the transition metals that are known for this characteristic.
Non-metallic elements usually have fewer oxidation states compared to transition metals and do not exhibit a wide range of oxidation states.
Transition elements are known for their ability to exhibit variable oxidation states due to the involvement of their d-orbitals in bonding.

questions.explanation: Transition elements, found in the d-block of the periodic table, are characterized by their ability to exhibit variable oxidation states. This is primarily due to the participation of d-electrons in bonding, allowing for a range of oxidation states. Normal elements typically have a fixed oxidation state, metallic elements as a broad category do not always show variability, and non-metallic elements generally have fewer oxidation states in comparison to transition metals.

questions.text: Which show maximum number of oxidation states in 3d series?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Manganese exhibits the highest number of oxidation states in the 3d series, ranging from +2 to +7.
Nickel typically shows fewer oxidation states, mainly +2 and sometimes +3.
Cobalt primarily exhibits +2 and +3 oxidation states.
Zinc predominantly exists in the +2 oxidation state and does not exhibit variability.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Mn (Manganese). Manganese is unique in the 3d transition series as it can exhibit a wide range of oxidation states from +2 to +7. This is due to its electronic configuration, which allows it to lose different numbers of electrons from both the 3d and 4s orbitals. In contrast, Ni (Nickel) predominantly shows +2 and occasionally +3 oxidation states, Co (Cobalt) usually exhibits +2 and +3 oxidation states, and Zn (Zinc) is restricted to the +2 oxidation state due to its fully filled d-orbital, making it less variable in terms of oxidation states.

questions.text: What type of bonding occurs in d-block elements?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ionic bonding typically occurs between metals and nonmetals, where electrons are transferred from one atom to another.
Covalent bonding occurs when atoms share electrons. D-block elements can form covalent bonds due to their ability to share d-electrons.
Metallic bonding involves a 'sea of electrons' that are delocalized among a lattice of metal cations. This is a characteristic feature of d-block elements.
D-block elements exhibit both covalent and metallic bonding. They can share electrons (covalent) and have delocalized electrons (metallic).

questions.explanation: D-block elements, also known as transition metals, exhibit both covalent and metallic bonding. The presence of d-electrons allows these elements to form covalent bonds by sharing electrons. Additionally, they exhibit metallic bonding due to the delocalization of electrons across a lattice of metal ions. This combination of bonding types is what gives d-block elements their unique properties. Ionic bonding, on the other hand, is not predominant in d-block elements, as it involves electron transfer rather than sharing or delocalization.

questions.text: What type of bonding occurs in d-block elements?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While some d-block elements can form ionic bonds, this is not the predominant type of bonding in these elements.
D-block elements can form covalent bonds, particularly in complex compounds, due to their ability to share electrons.
Metallic bonding is common in d-block elements, where electrons are delocalized across a lattice of metal atoms.
D-block elements exhibit both covalent and metallic bonding. Their unique electron configurations allow for shared electron pairs in covalent bonds and a 'sea of electrons' in metallic bonds.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Both B & C'. D-block elements, also known as transition metals, have partially filled d-orbitals that allow them to participate in both covalent and metallic bonding. Covalent bonding occurs when these elements share electrons, often seen in coordination compounds. Metallic bonding is characterized by the delocalization of electrons across the metal lattice, which is typical of metals, including many d-block elements. Ionic bonding, while possible in certain compounds, is not the primary bonding type in d-block elements.

questions.text: Metals are
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Metals are generally hard, but not all metals exhibit this property to the same extent.
Malleability is a key property of metals, allowing them to be hammered or pressed into shapes without breaking.
Ductility refers to a metal's ability to be drawn into wires, which is a common characteristic of metals.
Metals are generally hard, malleable, and ductile, embodying all these physical properties.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is All of the above. Metals typically exhibit a combination of properties such as hardness, malleability, and ductility. This makes them versatile and useful in various applications. While some metals may not be as hard as others, the combination of these properties is what characterizes a material as a metal. The other options are correct individually but do not encompass the full range of properties that define metals.

questions.text: Transition metal possess
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Transition metals often form colored compounds due to d-d electron transitions.
Transition metals are known for their ability to act as catalysts, facilitating chemical reactions.
Transition metals possess both characteristics: they often form colored compounds and have catalytic properties.
This option is incorrect as transition metals do exhibit definite colors and catalytic power.

questions.explanation: Transition metals are unique in that they often display vivid colors in their compounds due to electronic transitions within their d-orbitals. Additionally, they have significant catalytic power, which makes them effective in facilitating various chemical reactions. These properties are not mutually exclusive, which is why the correct answer is both A and B. Option D is incorrect because transition metals do exhibit these properties.

questions.text: Major achievement of CFT is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Interpreting the color of transition metal complexes
options.explanation: CFT helps explain why transition metal complexes are often colored due to d-d electron transitions.
CFT accounts for the adsorption spectra of complexes, detailing how light is absorbed and emitted.
CFT's major achievements include explaining both the color of transition metal complexes and their adsorption spectra.
This option is incorrect because CFT indeed explains both color and adsorption spectra of complexes.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C: Both A & B. Crystal Field Theory (CFT) is instrumental in explaining why transition metal complexes exhibit various colors and how they interact with light, leading to their characteristic adsorption spectra. Option A is partially correct, as CFT provides insights into the coloration of complexes by describing electron transitions within the d-orbitals. Option B is also partially correct, as CFT helps explain the adsorption spectra, which is crucial for understanding the electronic properties of these complexes. However, only Option C correctly identifies that both of these are major achievements of CFT. Option D is incorrect because both A and B are accurate reflections of CFT's contributions.

questions.text: Which are not considered member of d-block elements?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Zinc (Zn) has a completely filled d-sublevel in its elemental form, hence not typically considered a d-block element.
Cadmium (Cd) also has a completely filled d-sublevel, similar to zinc, which excludes it from being a true d-block element.
Mercury (Hg) has a fully filled d-sublevel and behaves differently from typical d-block elements.
All these elements have fully filled d-sublevels in their elemental forms, distinguishing them from other d-block elements.

questions.explanation: In the periodic table, d-block elements are typically characterized by an incomplete d-sublevel. However, zinc (Zn), cadmium (Cd), and mercury (Hg) have entirely filled d-orbitals in their elemental forms, which makes them exceptions to the usual properties of d-block elements. Thus, they are not considered true d-block elements, and the correct answer is 'All of the above.'

questions.text: Which of the following is non-typical transition metal?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Zinc is a non-typical transition metal because its common oxidation state (+2) has a fully filled 3d10 subshell, which limits its ability to exhibit variable oxidation states and complex ion formation.
Manganese is a typical transition metal, as it has a partially filled d-subshell (3d5) in its common oxidation states, allowing for multiple oxidation states and catalytic activity.
Chromium is a typical transition metal with a partially filled 3d-subshell (3d5), enabling it to display various oxidation states and participate in complex ion formation.
Cobalt is a typical transition metal with a partially filled d-subshell (3d7), which contributes to its ability to engage in complex ion formation and exhibit multiple oxidation states.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Zn. Zinc is classified as a non-typical transition metal because, in its most common oxidation state (+2), it has a completely filled 3d subshell (3d10). This electronic configuration results in a lack of typical transition metal properties, such as variable oxidation states and significant catalytic activity. In contrast, Mn, Cr, and Co are typical transition metals with partially filled d-subshells, allowing them to exhibit a variety of oxidation states, complex ion formation, and catalytic properties that are characteristic of transition metals.

questions.text: Unlike s-block elements d-block elements forms which compounds as well
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While d-block elements can form ionic compounds, the question asks about compounds formed in addition to those typically formed by s-block elements.
D-block elements are capable of forming covalent compounds due to the involvement of d orbitals, allowing them to share electrons with other atoms.
While d-block elements are known for forming coordinate compounds, the question focuses on the formation of covalent bonds, which is a broader category.
This option is invalid because d-block elements are known to form covalent compounds.

questions.explanation: D-block elements, unlike s-block elements, have the ability to form covalent compounds. This is primarily due to the presence of available d orbitals which allow them to engage in covalent bonding, sharing electrons with other atoms. Although d-block elements can also form ionic and coordinate compounds, covalent bonding is significant because it is not as common in s-block elements, which predominantly form ionic compounds. Therefore, the correct answer is 'Covalent compounds'. The other options are incorrect in the context of the question because they either describe bonding common to both s-block and d-block elements or do not accurately reflect the unique capabilities of d-block elements.

questions.text: Elements in which differentiating electron enters the (n-1)th d-orbitals of the (n-1)th main shell are called elements
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Elements in the s-block have their differentiating electron entering the s-orbital of the current shell.
Elements in the p-block have their differentiating electron entering the p-orbital of the current shell.
Elements in the d-block, also known as transition metals, have their differentiating electron entering the (n-1)d orbitals.
Elements in the f-block, also known as inner transition metals, have their differentiating electron entering the (n-2)f orbitals.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the d-block. These elements, also known as transition metals, have their differentiating electron entering the (n-1)d orbitals, which are part of the (n-1)th main shell. This is a key characteristic of the d-block elements in the periodic table. The s-block elements have their differentiating electron entering the s-orbital of the current main shell, not the (n-1)th shell. The p-block elements have their differentiating electron entering the p-orbital of the current main shell. The f-block elements, on the other hand, have their differentiating electron entering the (n-2)f orbitals, which is different from the d-block configuration.

questions.text: The trace metal present in insulin is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Manganese is involved in bone formation and enzyme reactions but is not a component of insulin.
Cobalt is a key element in vitamin B12, not in insulin.
Zinc plays a crucial role in the storage and stabilization of insulin.
Iron is essential for oxygen transport in blood but not involved in insulin.

questions.explanation: Insulin is a hormone that regulates blood glucose levels. Zinc (Zn) ions are critical for the proper storage and structural stability of insulin in the pancreas. Zinc helps in forming hexameric structures of insulin, which is how it is stored in the body. The other metals listed, such as manganese (Mn), cobalt (Co), and iron (Fe), play significant roles in different biological processes but are not involved in the function or structure of insulin.

questions.text: In which compound the oxidation 1 state of Mn is highest?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In KMnO4, Mn has an oxidation state of +7, which is the highest among the given options.
In MnO2, Mn has an oxidation state of +4.
In MnO, Mn has an oxidation state of +2.
This option is incorrect as one of the given compounds has a higher oxidation state for Mn.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is KMnO4. In KMnO4, potassium (K) has an oxidation state of +1, and the four oxygen atoms each have an oxidation state of -2, leading to a total of -8 from oxygen. To balance the overall charge of the compound, Mn must have an oxidation state of +7, which is the highest possible for Mn in these compounds. In MnO2, Mn has an oxidation state of +4, as each oxygen contributes -2. In MnO, Mn has an oxidation state of +2. Thus, KMnO4 has the highest oxidation state of Mn among the given options.

questions.text: The colour of Ni2+ ion is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Nickel(II) ions typically do not exhibit a blue color; this is more common for copper(II) ions.
This is the characteristic color of Ni2+ ions in solution, due to their specific electronic transitions.
While Ni2+ solutions are green, the term 'deep green' suggests a different or more intense shade than typically observed.
Nickel(II) ions do not exhibit an orange color. This color is more associated with compounds of other metals like dichromate ions.

questions.explanation: The color of a Ni2+ ion in solution is typically green. This is due to the d-d electronic transitions within the partly filled d8 electron configuration of Ni2+. The green color arises from the absorption of certain wavelengths of light and the reflection of green light. Other colors such as blue or orange are characteristic of other metal ions; for example, copper(II) ions often appear blue, while dichromate ions are orange. 'Deep green' is not a standard description for Ni2+ solutions, which are generally described as simply green.

questions.text: VBT is unable to to explain the nature of some of the complexes of
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: VBT consistently explains bonding in most cobalt complexes due to their electron configurations and typical ligand interactions.
Copper complexes often have notable deviations in their electronic structure that VBT cannot account for, particularly in cases involving Jahn-Teller distortions.
Nickel complexes are generally well-explained by VBT, as they often follow expected patterns of metal-ligand interactions.
While VBT can have limitations with certain high oxidation states of manganese, it generally explains their bonding effectively.

questions.explanation: Valence Bond Theory (VBT) is limited in its ability to describe the electronic structure of copper complexes due to the presence of Jahn-Teller distortions, which cause deviations from idealized geometries and electron distribution that VBT does not predict. In contrast, cobalt, nickel, and manganese complexes typically conform to the expectations set by VBT. For cobalt and nickel, the electron configurations and common oxidation states lead to predictable bonding patterns, while manganese may pose challenges in specific cases but is usually well-accounted for by VBT.

questions.text: The color of transition metals is due to
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is correct because transition metals have partially filled d-orbitals, and the electronic transitions between these d-orbitals cause the absorption of certain wavelengths of light, giving rise to color.
This is incorrect as 'n-ii transitions' is not a recognized term in the context of electron transitions in chemistry.
This is incorrect because ionization refers to the process of losing or gaining electrons to form ions, which does not directly cause the color of transition metals.
This is incorrect. While the loss of s electrons can occur in transition metals, it is not responsible for their color. The color is due to d-d electronic transitions.

questions.explanation: The color of transition metals is primarily due to d-d transitions. These transitions involve the movement of electrons between different energy levels within the d-orbitals. When visible light is absorbed by these metals, certain wavelengths are absorbed while others are reflected or transmitted, resulting in the observed color. Option B is incorrect because 'n-ii transitions' is not a valid term in this context. Option C is incorrect because ionization involves electron removal, not responsible for color. Option D is incorrect because the loss of an s electron affects the oxidation state but not the color directly.

questions.text: VBT does not explain
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: VBT does not account for the electronic transitions responsible for the absorption spectra of transition metals.
The color of transition metal ions is linked to d-d transitions, which VBT does not explain.
VBT does not provide a comprehensive explanation for the thermodynamics of bond formation such as heat of formation.
VBT fails to explain all of these phenomena: absorption spectra, color of transition metal ions, and heat of formation.

questions.explanation: The Valence Bond Theory (VBT) focuses primarily on the formation of coordination bonds through the overlap of atomic orbitals. However, it does not adequately explain several important properties of coordination compounds and transition metals. This includes the absorption spectra and color, which are better explained by the Crystal Field Theory (CFT) and Ligand Field Theory (LFT) that consider electronic transitions and energy levels. Similarly, VBT does not provide detailed insights into the thermodynamic aspect of bond formation, such as the heat of formation. Thus, the correct answer is that VBT does not explain any of the phenomena listed in the options.

questions.text: Which of the following cations has maximum number of unpaired electrons?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Fe2+ has the electronic configuration [Ar] 3d6, which means it has 4 unpaired electrons.
Co2+ has the electronic configuration [Ar] 3d7, resulting in 3 unpaired electrons.
Mn2+ has the electronic configuration [Ar] 3d5, with 5 unpaired electrons, the maximum among these options.
Ni2+ has the electronic configuration [Ar] 3d8, which contains 2 unpaired electrons.

questions.explanation: The electronic configurations for the given cations are as follows: Fe2+ is [Ar] 3d6 with 4 unpaired electrons, Co2+ is [Ar] 3d7 with 3 unpaired electrons, Mn2+ is [Ar] 3d5 with 5 unpaired electrons, and Ni2+ is [Ar] 3d8 with 2 unpaired electrons. Mn2+ has the highest number of unpaired electrons, hence it is the correct answer. The other cations have fewer unpaired electrons due to the way electrons fill the d-orbitals according to Hund's rule.

questions.text: On the basis of CFT the bonding between the metal and ligand is totally
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: According to CFT, the interaction between the metal and ligand is considered ionic due to electrostatic attractions.
CFT does not account for covalent bonding as it focuses on electrostatic interactions.
Although coordination involves metal-ligand interactions, CFT specifically models these as ionic rather than coordinate bonds.
Metallic bonding is not applicable to metal-ligand interactions in the context of CFT.

questions.explanation: In Crystal Field Theory (CFT), the bonding between a metal and a ligand is described as ionic. This is because CFT models the interaction as purely electrostatic, where the metal cation is attracted to the negatively charged field produced by the ligands. The theory does not consider any covalent or shared electron characteristics, which would be necessary to describe covalent or coordinate bonds. Metallic bonding refers to a different type of bonding prevalent in solid metals, involving a 'sea of electrons', which is not relevant in the context of CFT.

questions.text: CFT can very well explain
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: CFT explains the color of transition metal complexes due to the splitting of d-orbitals and the absorption of specific wavelengths of light.
CFT accounts for magnetic properties by considering the electron arrangement and spin states in split d-orbitals.
The spectra of transition metals can be explained by CFT through d-d transitions and electronic excitations.
CFT can explain all of these aspects: color, magnetic properties, and spectra, as they are interrelated through the splitting of d-orbitals in transition metal complexes.

questions.explanation: Crystal Field Theory (CFT) is a model that describes the electronic structure of transition metal complexes. It explains how the ligands surrounding a metal ion affect the energy levels of the metal's d-orbitals. This orbital splitting leads to absorption of certain wavelengths of light, which accounts for the color of the complexes. Additionally, the arrangement of electrons in these split d-orbitals determines the magnetic properties of the complexes, and the transitions between these energy levels are responsible for the observed spectra. Therefore, CFT can explain color, magnetic properties, and spectra of transition metal complexes, making 'All' the correct answer.

questions.text: In group theory, the triply degenerate set is denoted by
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The eg set is doubly degenerate, not triply degenerate. It refers to orbitals in octahedral complexes.
The t2g set is triply degenerate and commonly seen in octahedral complexes.
The e2g notation does not correspond to a standard designation in crystal field theory.
The tg notation is incorrect and does not refer to any standard degenerate set.

questions.explanation: In the context of group theory and crystal field theory, the t2g set is triply degenerate. It refers to three orbitals that have the same energy level in an octahedral field, which are typically the dxy, dyz, and dxz orbitals. The incorrect options are: eg, which is doubly degenerate and represents dz2 and dx2-y2 orbitals; e2g, which is not a recognized term in this context; and tg, which is not used in standard notation for degenerate sets.

questions.text: The energy gap between t₂g and eg sets in denoted by
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Δο (Delta o) refers to the crystal field splitting energy in octahedral complexes.
10 Dq is another way of expressing the crystal field splitting energy in octahedral complexes, where Dq is the energy difference between t₂g and eg orbitals.
Both Δo and 10 Dq refer to the same energy difference in octahedral complexes, hence this option is correct.
1 Dq represents a smaller unit of measurement within the 10 Dq structure, not the entire energy gap.

questions.explanation: The energy gap between the t₂g and eg sets in an octahedral complex can be denoted either by Δo or 10 Dq. Δo (Delta o) is the symbol commonly used to represent the crystal field splitting energy. 10 Dq is a specific unit of measurement for this energy gap, with 10 Dq being equal to Δo. Therefore, the correct answer is 'Both A & B.' Option D, 1 Dq, only represents a fraction of the total energy gap, not the entirety.

questions.text: Aºor 10 Dq is called crystal field
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is a general term and does not specifically describe the splitting of d-orbitals in a crystal field.
Correct. Crystal field splitting energy refers to the energy difference between the split d-orbitals in a crystal field.
This term is related to the overall stabilization of a complex due to ligand interactions, but it does not specifically refer to the energy difference between split d-orbitals.
This option is incorrect as one of the given choices correctly describes the term.

questions.explanation: Crystal field theory explains how the degeneracy of d-orbitals is broken in a transition metal complex due to the approach of ligands. The term 10 Dq, also known as the crystal field splitting energy, represents the energy difference between the higher and lower sets of d-orbitals. Option B is correct because it specifically identifies this energy difference. Option A is too general, Option C refers to a related but distinct concept, and Option D is incorrect as the correct term is provided.

questions.text: The common ligands can be arranged in order of their increasing splitting power to cause d-orbitals splitting. This series is called as
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The electro-chemical series relates to electrode potentials, not ligand field splitting.
Correct! The spectrochemical series arranges ligands based on their splitting power in transition metal complexes.
Physico-chemical properties are broader and do not specifically focus on ligand field splitting.
This term is not used in the context of ligand field splitting in chemistry.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Spectro-chemical. The spectrochemical series is a list of ligands ordered by their ability to split the d-orbitals of a central metal ion in a coordination complex. This determines the color and magnetic properties of the complex. The other options are incorrect because they either refer to different chemical concepts (electro-chemical and physico-chemical) or do not exist in this context (spectro-electrical).

questions.text: Coordination compound show
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Structural isomerism involves different connectivity of atoms within the compound, but it doesn't account for all isomerism types in coordination compounds.
Stereo-isomerism involves different spatial arrangements of atoms, but this alone doesn't cover all types of isomerism in coordination compounds.
Coordination compounds can exhibit both structural and stereo-isomerism, encompassing a wide range of isomeric behaviors.
This option is incorrect because coordination compounds do exhibit both structural and stereo-isomerism.

questions.explanation: Coordination compounds are known for their ability to exhibit various types of isomerism. Structural isomerism occurs when the connectivity of atoms differs within the compound, leading to different structures, such as linkage isomers, ionization isomers, and coordination isomers. Stereo-isomerism, on the other hand, involves the same connectivity but different spatial arrangements, such as geometric isomers and optical isomers.Thus, the correct answer is Both A & B because coordination compounds can show both types of isomerism. Options A and B are partially correct but incomplete on their own, while Option D is incorrect because it ignores the isomerism exhibited by coordination compounds.

questions.text: According to CFT, the metal ligand bond is considered to be ionic to percentage
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In Crystal Field Theory, the metal-ligand bond is considered purely ionic, meaning 100% ionic character.
While metal-ligand bonds have significant ionic character in CFT, this option underestimates the theoretical assumption of 100% ionic character.
This option also underestimates the ionic character assumed in CFT. In theory, metal-ligand bonds are considered 100% ionic in nature.
This option significantly underestimates the ionic character of metal-ligand bonds according to CFT.

questions.explanation: Crystal Field Theory (CFT) assumes that the interaction between metal ions and ligands is purely electrostatic and therefore considers the metal-ligand bond to be 100% ionic. This simplification helps to model the effect of ligands on the d-orbitals of metal ions without considering covalent character. Options B (90%), C (80%), and D (70%) suggest a partial covalent character, which is not in line with the theoretical assumptions of CFT.

questions.text: Arrange the following oxide of chromium in increasing acidic character:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This order incorrectly suggests that CrO has a higher acidic character than Cr₂O3 and CrO3, which is not true.
CrO3 has the highest oxidation state and is the most acidic, followed by Cr2O3, with CrO being basic.
This order incorrectly places Cr₂O3 and CrO as more acidic than CrO3, which is incorrect.
This order incorrectly suggests CrO is more acidic than Cr2O3, which is not true.

questions.explanation: The acidity of metal oxides generally increases with the oxidation state of the metal. In chromium oxides, CrO3 has the highest oxidation state (+6) and is the most acidic. Cr2O3 has an intermediate oxidation state (+3) and is amphoteric, showing both acidic and basic properties. CrO, with the lowest oxidation state (+2), is basic. Therefore, the correct order of increasing acidic character is CrO < Cr2O3 < CrO3.

questions.text: Many hexaaqua complex ions can undergo reaction with water as given below [Fe(H₂O)+6]+2 aq + H₂O = [Fe(H2o)5, OH+ H3O+The reaction is classed as:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A redox reaction involves the transfer of electrons, resulting in changes in oxidation states. In this reaction, no such changes occur.
An acid-base reaction involves the transfer of protons. In this reaction, water acts as a base, accepting a proton to form H3O+, and the complex ion acts as an acid, donating a proton.
A decomposition reaction involves a single compound breaking down into two or more simpler substances. This is not the primary process occurring here.

A substitution reaction involves the replacement of one group in a molecule with another. Although the water molecule is replaced by a hydroxide ion, the reaction is more accurately described as acid-base.

questions.explanation: The reaction between [Fe(H₂O)6]2+ and water is classified as an acid-base reaction. In this process, the hexaaqua complex ion donates a proton (H+) to a water molecule, resulting in the formation of [Fe(H₂O)5(OH)]+ and H3O+. This exchange of protons is characteristic of acid-base reactions. The other options are incorrect because there is no transfer of electrons (redox), no breaking down into simpler substances (decomposition), and while there is a substitution of water by hydroxide, the main feature of the reaction is proton transfer.

questions.text: The compound of Cr (Chromium) with a strong reducing power is;
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) is a strong oxidizing agent, not a reducing agent.
Chromium(I) chloride (CrCl) has a low oxidation state, making it a strong reducing agent as it can easily donate electrons.
Chromium(III) oxide (Cr2O3) is generally stable and does not exhibit strong reducing properties.
This option is incorrect as CrCl is indeed a compound of Chromium with a strong reducing power.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is CrCl. Chromium in CrCl is in a low oxidation state (+1), which makes it more likely to donate electrons and thus act as a strong reducing agent. In contrast, K2Cr2O7 is a strong oxidizing agent due to Chromium being in a high oxidation state. Cr2O3 is in a stable oxidation state (+3) and does not typically display strong reducing properties.

questions.text: The diamagnetic substance are
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Weakly repelled by a strong magnetic field
options.explanation: Diamagnetic substances are weakly repelled by all magnetic fields, not just strong ones.

options.text: Strongly repelled by a weak magnetic field
options.explanation: Diamagnetic substances are not strongly repelled; the repulsion is generally weak.

options.text: Strongly repelled by a weak magnetic field
options.explanation: Diamagnetic substances are weakly, not strongly, repelled by magnetic fields.
This option correctly describes diamagnetic substances, which are repelled by all types of magnetic fields.

questions.explanation: Diamagnetic substances are characterized by having no unpaired electrons and thus do not have a net magnetic moment.
