options.explanation: Lanthanoids contraction leads to a decrease in atomic size across the lanthanide series, increasing effective nuclear charge. However, this contraction causes Pb to have a higher ionization potential compared to Sn, not lower. Thus, the statement is misleading in context, but generally considered true regarding the effect of lanthanoids contraction.

options.text: If successive ionization energies are 332, 738, 849, 4080, 4958 kJ/mol, then this element can belong to Group 13.
options.explanation: The sharp increase in ionization energy after the removal of the third electron indicates that the element likely has three valence electrons, suggesting that it belongs to Group 13. Therefore, this statement is true.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: Cr2+(g) ion has a greater magnetic moment compared to Co3+(g). This is because Cr2+ has four unpaired electrons, resulting in a higher magnetic moment compared to Co3+, which has three unpaired electrons. The magnetic moment is directly related to the number of unpaired electrons.Option B is incorrect because it falsely claims that the ionization potential of an iron anion equals its electron gain enthalpy. These are distinct processes with different energy values.Option C is misleading in context, as lanthanoids contraction increases ionization potential, but Pb has a higher ionization potential than Sn due to this effect.Option D is true because the large increase in ionization energy after the third electron removal indicates the presence of three valence electrons, characteristic of Group 13 elements.

questions.text: The 3d metal ions form coloured compounds because the energy corresponding to the following lies in the visible range of electromagnetic spectrum
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Free energy change of complex formation by 3d metal ions
options.explanation: The free energy change of complex formation may influence the stability of the complex, but it does not directly contribute to the color formation. Hence, this option is incorrect.

The energy corresponding to the d-d transition of 3d electrons lies in the visible range of the electromagnetic spectrum. When a photon of light with the same energy as the d-d transition is absorbed, an electron in one of the 3d orbitals of the metal ion is excited to a higher energy level. This causes the ion to absorb a certain wavelength of light, which is why transition metal compounds appear colored.

options.text: The heat of hydration of 3d metal ions
options.explanation: The heat of hydration of 3d metal ions is the amount of heat released when one mole of a 3d metal ion is hydrated in water. Hence, this option is incorrect.

options.text: The ionization energy of 3d metal ions
options.explanation: The ionization energy refers to the energy required to remove an electron from a neutral atom or ion. While the ionization energy may influence the electronic structure of the metal ion, it does not directly cause color formation. Hence, this option is incorrect

questions.explanation: The energy corresponding to the d-d transition of 3d electrons lies in the visible range of the electromagnetic spectrum. When a photon of light with the same energy as the d-d transition is absorbed, an electron in one of the 3d orbitals of the metal ion is excited to a higher energy level. This causes the ion to absorb a certain wavelength of light, which is why transition metal compounds appear colored.

questions.text: Which of substances can produce an acidic solution?I. Na+II. Fe3+III. CI-
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Na+ is a group 1 cation and does not hydrolyze in water to produce H+ ions.
Fe3+ can hydrolyze in water, releasing H+ ions and contributing to an acidic solution.
Na+ does not contribute to acidity as it does not release H+ ions, so this combination is incorrect.
While Fe3+ can produce acidity, Cl- is a spectator ion and does not contribute to acidity.
Only Fe3+ can cause a solution to become acidic. Na+ and Cl- do not affect the pH of the solution.

questions.explanation: An acidic solution is formed when a substance releases H+ ions in water. Fe3+ (Iron(III) ion) can hydrolyze in water to release H+ ions, thus contributing to acidity. Na+ (Sodium ion) is a group 1 cation and does not hydrolyze with water, so it does not affect the pH. Cl- (Chloride ion) is the conjugate base of a strong acid (HCl) and acts as a spectator ion, not altering the pH. Therefore, the correct answer is II only.

questions.text: In [Co(C2O4)3]4- , the metal ion has a coordination number of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: As per calculations (refer to the calculation below) the correct answer is 6. Hence, this option is incorrect.
As per calculations (refer to the calculation below) the correct answer is 6. Hence, this option is incorrect.
As per calculations (refer to the calculation below) the correct answer is 6. Hence, this option is incorrect.

The coordination number refers to the number of bonds formed between a central metal ion and the ligands surrounding it in a coordination complex. In the above Complex, 3 C2O4 are bound to the Co. C2O4 are bidentate ligands (donate two pairs of electrons to the metal ion thus each is bound to the metal ion at two different places), thus the Coordination number is 3 x 2 = 6.

questions.explanation: The coordination number refers to the number of bonds formed between a central metal ion and the ligands surrounding it in a coordination complex. In the above Complex, 3 C2O4 are bound to the Co. C2O4 are bidentate ligands (donate two pairs of electrons to the metal ion thus each is bound to the metal ion at two different places), thus the Coordination number is 3 x 2 = 6.

questions.text: FeSO4.7H2O is also known as:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Blue vitriol is the common name for copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4.5H2O), not iron(II) sulfate heptahydrate.
Green vitriol is the common name for iron(II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4.7H2O) due to its characteristic pale green crystals.
White vitriol refers to zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4.7H2O), not iron(II) sulfate heptahydrate.
Mohr's salt is ammonium iron(II) sulfate hexahydrate (FeSO4.(NH4)2SO4.6H2O), which is different from iron(II) sulfate heptahydrate.

questions.explanation: Green vitriol is the correct answer because it is the common name for iron(II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4.7H2O), which appears as pale green crystals. Blue vitriol is incorrectly associated with this compound as it refers to copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate. White vitriol is zinc sulfate heptahydrate, while Mohr's salt is a different compound altogether, being ammonium iron(II) sulfate hexahydrate.

questions.text: Oxidation number of Cr in the complex [Cr(H2O)4Cl2]Cl is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The complex ion is [Cr(H2O)4Cl2]+ because the chloride ion outside the square brackets has a -1 charge, making the complex ion balance it with a +1 charge. Water (H2O) is a neutral ligand with an oxidation state of 0, and chloride (Cl) inside the complex has an oxidation state of -1. Let x be the oxidation state of Cr. Then, x + 4(0) + 2(-1) = +1. This simplifies to x - 2 = +1, so x = +3.
If the oxidation state of Cr were +2, the charge on the complex ion would be 2 + 4(0) + 2(-1) = 0, which would result in a neutral complex, not balanced by the outer Cl-
If the oxidation state of Cr were +1, the charge on the complex ion would be 1 + 4(0) + 2(-1) = -1, resulting in a complex ion [Cr(H2O)4Cl2]-, which would require a positive counterion.
If the oxidation state of Cr were +6, the charge on the complex ion would be 6 + 4(0) + 2(-1) = +4, which is inconsistent with the given formula

questions.explanation: The complex ion is [Cr(H2O)4Cl2]+ because the chloride ion outside the square brackets has a -1 charge, making the complex ion balance it with a +1 charge. Water (H2O) is a neutral ligand with an oxidation state of 0, and chloride (Cl) inside the complex has an oxidation state of -1. Let x be the oxidation state of Cr. Then, x + 4(0) + 2(-1) = +1. This simplifies to x - 2 = +1, so x = +3.

questions.text: Cobalt, nickel and iron of first transition series can be magnetized hence they are called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While paramagnetic substances are attracted to magnetic fields due to unpaired electrons, the attraction is weaker compared to ferromagnetic materials.
Diamagnetic substances are repelled by magnetic fields because all their electrons are paired, which is not the case for Co, Ni, and Fe.
Ferromagnetic materials like iron, cobalt, and nickel are strongly attracted to magnetic fields and can retain magnetization due to the alignment of their magnetic domains.
This option is incorrect because cobalt, nickel, and iron are well-known for their ferromagnetic properties.

questions.explanation: Ferromagnetic materials, such as iron, cobalt, and nickel, have the ability to be strongly attracted to magnetic fields and to retain magnetization even when the external field is removed. This property is due to the alignment of magnetic domains within these materials. In contrast, paramagnetic substances are only weakly attracted to magnetic fields due to the presence of unpaired electrons, and diamagnetic materials are repelled because all their electrons are paired. Therefore, the correct classification for cobalt, nickel, and iron is ferromagnetic.

questions.text: The following represents the main reaction occurring when dilute HNO3 is added to Cu turnings. The value of X and Y are:﻿X Cu + Y HNO3 → 3 Cu(NO3)2 + 4 H2O + 2 NO
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Balancing the equation with X=1 and Y=4 gives: Cu + 4 HNO3 → 3 Cu(NO3)2 + 4 H2O + 2 NO. This is not balanced for Cu and N.

Balancing the equation with X=3 and Y=8 gives: 3 Cu + 8 HNO3 → 3 Cu(NO3)2 + 4 H2O + 2 NO.Copper (Cu): 3 on both sides.Hydrogen (H): 8 on the left, 8 on the right.Nitrogen (N): 8 on the left, 6 + 2 = 8 on the right.Oxygen (O): 24 on the left, 18 + 4 + 2 = 24 on the right. This equation is balanced.
Balancing with X=5 and Y=6 does not yield a balanced equation.
Balancing with X=7 and Y=1 does not yield a balanced equation.

questions.explanation: Balancing the equation with X=3 and Y=8 gives: 3 Cu + 8 HNO3 → 3 Cu(NO3)2 + 4 H2O + 2 NO.Copper (Cu): 3 on both sides.Hydrogen (H): 8 on the left, 8 on the right.Nitrogen (N): 8 on the left, 6 + 2 = 8 on the right.Oxygen (O): 24 on the left, 18 + 4 + 2 = 24 on the right. This equation is balanced.

questions.text: On heating crystals of copper sulphate become white. This is because:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: White anhydrous copper sulphate is formed
options.explanation: Heating removes the water of crystallization from hydrated copper(II) sulfate (CuSO4.5H2O), resulting in the formation of white anhydrous copper(II) sulfate (CuSO4).

options.text: There is a change in crystalline structure
options.explanation: While the dehydration process does change the crystal structure, this is not the main reason for the color change; the loss of water molecules is the primary factor affecting color.
Copper sulfide is not formed when copper sulfate is heated in air under normal conditions; this requires specific reducing conditions and sulfur presence.

options.text: Copper is formed which is oxidized to Copper oxide
options.explanation: Heating copper sulfate may lead to decomposition at very high temperatures, but the initial color change to white is due to dehydration, not the reduction to copper metal.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that white anhydrous copper(II) sulfate is formed. Hydrated copper(II) sulfate (CuSO4.5H2O) is blue due to the presence of water molecules coordinated to the Cu(2+) ion. When heated, it loses these water molecules, forming anhydrous copper(II) sulfate (CuSO4), which is a white powder. The other options are incorrect because they either describe processes that do not occur under the described conditions (such as the formation of copper sulfide or copper metal) or describe secondary effects (such as changes in crystal structure) that do not primarily cause the color change.

questions.text: d-block elements generally form:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Covalent hydrides are typically formed by p-block elements, as they tend to share electrons with hydrogen to form covalent bonds.
Complex hydrides are usually formed by alkali and alkaline earth metals, often involving metal halides, such as in LiAlH4 and NaBH4.

Many d-block metals can absorb hydrogen into the interstitial sites of their crystal lattices, forming non-stoichiometric compounds known as interstitial hydrides or metallic hydrides. This occurs because the d-block metals have open interstitial sites in their crystal structures that can accommodate hydrogen atoms.
Salt-like or ionic hydrides are formed by highly electropositive s-block elements, such as sodium and calcium, forming ionic bonds with hydrogen (e.g., NaH, CaH2).

questions.explanation: Interstitial hydrides are the correct answer because d-block metals have crystal lattices with interstitial sites that can accommodate hydrogen atoms, leading to the formation of non-stoichiometric compounds. Covalent hydrides are incorrect as they are typically formed by p-block elements. Complex hydrides are incorrect because they are usually associated with alkali and alkaline earth metals. Salt-like hydrides are incorrect as they are formed by s-block elements with hydrogen, resulting in ionic compounds.

questions.text: The extraction of Pig iron is carried out in a
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A blast furnace is essential for the extraction of pig iron. It operates by reducing iron ore into molten iron, also known as pig iron, using a combination of high temperatures and reducing agents.
Used mainly for non-ferrous metal extraction and roasting of ores, the reverberatory furnace is not used for extracting pig iron from iron ore.
Heroult's furnace, or electric arc furnace, is primarily used in steelmaking processes to melt and refine steel rather than extracting pig iron from iron ore.
Bessemer's converter is utilized in converting pig iron into steel by removing impurities, but it does not play a role in the initial extraction of pig iron.

questions.explanation: The blast furnace is the correct answer as it is specifically designed for the extraction of pig iron by reducing iron ores using high temperatures and reducing agents like coke. Other furnaces listed, such as the reverberatory furnace, Heroult's electric furnace, and Bessemer's furnace, serve different purposes in metallurgy and are not suitable for the initial extraction of pig iron from ore.

questions.text: Which occurs in nature in a free state:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Iron (Fe) is a highly reactive metal that readily forms compounds. It is commonly found as oxides or sulfides, not in a free state.
Calcium (Ca) is a reactive alkaline earth metal that is always found in nature as part of compounds such as carbonates and sulfates, never in a free state.
Aluminum (Al) is a reactive metal that is usually found in nature as aluminum oxide or in silicate minerals, and not in a free state due to its affinity for oxygen.
Platinum (Pt) is a noble metal known for its low reactivity. It can be found in its native state in nature, often in placer deposits alongside other platinum group metals.

questions.explanation: Platinum (Pt) is the correct answer because it is a noble metal with low reactivity, allowing it to exist in nature in its free state. Other metals like Iron (Fe), Calcium (Ca), and Aluminum (Al) are too reactive to be found in their free states naturally. These metals typically form stable compounds with other elements, such as oxides and sulfides, due to their high reactivity.

questions.text: Crude form of copper obtained by Bessemerization has blisters on its surface due to:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While carbon can be present as an impurity, the blisters are not primarily due to the release of carbon dioxide.
Water vapor release is not the main cause of blisters in the copper obtained by Bessemerization.
The presence of various impurities contributes to the overall nature of the crude copper, but the blisters are specifically linked to the oxidation of a particular impurity.

Copper ores often contain sulfur. During Bessemerization (or a similar process called smelting in a reverberatory furnace for copper), the sulfur is oxidized to sulfur dioxide (SO2), which escapes as gas and causes blisters on the surface of the solidified molten copper. This blistered copper is about 98% pure.

questions.explanation: Copper ores often contain sulfur. During Bessemerization (or a similar process called smelting in a reverberatory furnace for copper), the sulfur is oxidized to sulfur dioxide (SO2), which escapes as gas and causes blisters on the surface of the solidified molten copper. This blistered copper is about 98% pure.

questions.text: Which of the following elements has highest electrical conductivity:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Zinc is a moderately good conductor of electricity, but it is not the best among the options given.
Iron is a fair conductor of electricity. It is used in some applications but is less conductive compared to copper and silver.
Copper is an excellent conductor of electricity, making it one of the most commonly used metals in electrical wiring. However, it is not the best among the options given.
Silver has the highest electrical conductivity of all metals. Despite its superior conductivity, its high cost makes it less commonly used than copper.

questions.explanation: Silver (Ag) has the highest electrical conductivity among all metals, making it the best option for conducting electricity. This property makes silver ideal for high-performance applications where conductivity is critical. However, due to its cost, copper (Cu) is more frequently used in electrical wiring despite being slightly less conductive. Zinc (Zn) and iron (Fe) are both less conductive than copper and silver, which is why they are not favored for applications requiring high electrical conductivity.

questions.text: The Matte is an impure substance of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Iron is not typically associated with the term 'matte' in metallurgy. Matte formation is more relevant to copper extraction.
Aluminum extraction does not involve matte; it primarily involves the electrolysis of alumina (Al₂O₃).
Matte is an intermediate product in copper extraction from sulfide ores, mostly composed of copper(I) sulfide (Cu₂S) and iron(II) sulfide (FeS).
Zinc extraction does not typically result in the formation of matte; it involves roasting and reduction or electrolysis.

questions.explanation: Matte is an intermediate product in the extraction of copper from sulfide ores, characterized by a molten mixture primarily consisting of copper(I) sulfide (Cu₂S) and iron(II) sulfide (FeS). This occurs during the smelting stage of copper extraction. In contrast, the extraction processes for iron, aluminum, and zinc do not involve the formation of matte. Iron extraction involves reduction in a blast furnace, aluminum relies on electrolytic processes, and zinc extraction typically involves roasting followed by reduction or electrolysis.

questions.text: The substance used in the extraction of iron from hematite in the blast furnace is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Calcium phosphate is not utilized in the blast furnace process for extracting iron. It does not contribute to removing impurities or aiding in iron extraction.

Limestone acts as a flux in the blast furnace. It decomposes to calcium oxide (CaO), which reacts with silica (SiO2) impurities in the ore to form calcium silicate (CaSiO3) slag, thus aiding in impurity removal.
Silica is an impurity found in the iron ore that must be removed during processing. It is not added to the furnace for extraction purposes.
Common salt does not play a role in the blast furnace process for extracting iron from hematite.

questions.explanation: Limestone is crucial in the blast furnace as it serves as a flux. At high temperatures, limestone (calcium carbonate) decomposes into calcium oxide and carbon dioxide. The calcium oxide reacts with silica impurities in the iron ore to form calcium silicate slag, which is removed from the furnace, thus purifying the iron. Calcium phosphate and common salt are not involved in this process, and silica is an impurity that needs to be removed, not a substance added for the extraction.

questions.text: The purest form of iron is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Cast iron contains a high carbon content (typically 2-4%) and various impurities, making it far from pure.
Wrought iron has a very low carbon content (less than 0.08%) with minimal impurities, making it the purest commercially available form of iron.
Hard steel is an alloy with variable carbon content and additional elements for strength, not pure iron.
Stainless steel is an alloy that includes chromium, which gives it corrosion resistance, and is not pure iron.

questions.explanation: Wrought iron is the purest form of iron commercially available due to its low carbon content (less than 0.08%) and minimal impurities. Although it contains silicate slag inclusions, these do not significantly affect its iron purity. Cast iron, on the other hand, has a high carbon content and impurities, making it less pure. Hard steel and stainless steel are alloys with various additional elements, including carbon and chromium, respectively, which prevent them from being considered pure forms of iron.

questions.text: Iron obtained from blast furnace is known as:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Cast iron is made by remelting pig iron and often includes scrap iron and other alloys. It is not directly obtained from the blast furnace.
Wrought iron has a very low carbon content and is produced through different processes such as puddling, not directly from a blast furnace.
Pig iron is the direct product obtained from smelting iron ore in a blast furnace and contains a high percentage of carbon (3.5-4.5%) and other impurities.
Steel is produced by further processing pig iron to reduce carbon content and add other elements. It is not directly obtained from a blast furnace.

questions.explanation: Pig iron is the intermediate product obtained directly from the blast furnace during the smelting of iron ore. It contains a high carbon content and other impurities, making it the precursor to other forms of iron such as cast iron and steel. Cast iron is derived from pig iron by remelting it, while wrought iron is produced through processes that involve reducing carbon content. Steel is made by refining pig iron to lower its carbon content and adding other elements.

questions.text: Potassium permanganate reacts with ferrous sulfate as follows.Which of the following statements is TRUE for the given reaction? I. Iron (II) ions are reduced to iron (III) ions. II. Iron (II) ions lose an electron to form iron (III) ions. III. The oxidation state of manganese changes from +7 to +2. IV. Permanganate ions are reduced by the gain of 2 electrons.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Statement I is incorrect as the change of oxidation number from +2 to +3 is oxidation and not reduction. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Statement I is incorrect as the change of oxidation number from +2 to +3 is oxidation and not reduction, statement IV is also incorrect as permangnate ions are reduced by the gain of 5 electrons. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Statement II is correct as the loss of one electron will add +1 to the oxidation number so, +2 --> +3. Statement III is also correct as the oxidation number of Manganese in KMnO4 is +7. (4x-2) + (+1) = -7 so to balance this into zero net charges the oxidation number of Mn is +7. The charge of Mn in MnSO4 is +2 as the charge of SO4 is -2 so both add up to 0 net charge. Hence, this option is correct.
Statement IV is also incorrect as permangnate ions are reduced by the gain of 5 electrons. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Statement II is correct as the loss of one electron will add +1 to the oxidation number so, +2 --> +3. Statement III is also correct as the oxidation number of Manganese in KMnO4 is +7. (4x-2) + (+1) = -7 so to balance this into zero net charges the oxidation number of Mn is +7. The charge of Mn in MnSO4 is +2 as the charge of SO4 is -2 so both add up to 0 net charge. Hence, this option is correct.

questions.text: Which of the following options is CORRECT for [Ni(NH2CH2CH2NH2)3]2+
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Coordination Number: 6 Type of Ligand: Bidentate
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The coordination number refers to the total number of coordinate bonds formed between the central metal and the ligands. Each NH2CH2CH2NH2 is a bidentate ligand, meaning it can donate two pairs of electrons to the metal center. With three such ligands, the total coordination number is 3 x 2 = 6.

options.text: Coordination Number: 6 Type of Ligand: Hexadentate
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Though the coordination number is correctly identified as 6, NH2CH2CH2NH2 is not a hexadentate ligand. It is a bidentate ligand as it has only two donor atoms.

options.text: Coordination Number: 3 Type of Ligand: Bidentate
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While NH2CH2CH2NH2 is indeed a bidentate ligand, the coordination number should be 6, not 3. Each ligand forms two bonds with the metal, resulting in a coordination number of 3 x 2 = 6.

This option is incorrect. NH2CH2CH2NH2 is a bidentate ligand, not hexadentate, and the coordination number should be 6, not 3. Each ligand forms two bonds, leading to a coordination number of 3 x 2 = 6.

questions.explanation: In the complex [Ni(NH2CH2CH2NH2)3]2+, the nickel center is surrounded by three NH2CH2CH2NH2 ligands. Each of these ligands is bidentate, meaning it can form two coordinate covalent bonds with the nickel ion. Therefore, the total coordination number for this complex is 6 (3 ligands x 2 bonds each). Option A correctly identifies both the coordination number and the type of ligand. Options B, C, and D are incorrect either because they misidentify the coordination number or the nature of the ligand.

questions.text: If 80 grams of Al and 80 grams of Fe2O3 are combined, what is the maximum number of moles of Fe that can be produced?2Al(s) + Fe2O3 (s) → Al2O3 (s) + 2Fe(s)
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: As per calculations (refer to the calculation below) the correct answer is 1. Hence, this option is incorrect.

To solve this question we will first calculate the moles of Al and Fe2O3 to find out which one of these reactants is the limiting reagent, and then use molar ratio to find the moles of Fe. To find the moles we use the formula: Moles = Mass / Molecular massMoles of Al: Mass: 80g; Molecular mass: 27Moles=80 / 27Moles = 3Moles of Fe2O3: Mass: 80g; Molecular mass: 56Moles = 80 / 160Moles=0.5So, as the moles of Fe2O3 are less than the moles of Al (0.5 < 3.0), the limiting reagent is Fe2O3, and hence the molar ratio of Fe2O3 and Fe is used to find the moles of Fe. Fe2O3 : Fe 1 : 2 0.5 : xWhere x = unknown number of moles of FeBy solving this by cross multiplication the number of moles of Fe come out to be 1.0 and therefore the answer is B.
As per calculations (refer to the calculation below) the correct answer is 1. Hence, this option is incorrect.

To find the moles we use the formula: Moles = Mass / Molecular mass Moles of Al: Mass: 80g; Molecular mass: 27 Moles=80 / 27 Moles = 3 Moles of Fe2O3: Mass: 80g; Molecular mass: 56 Moles = 80 / 160 Moles=0.5 So, as the moles of Fe2O3 are less than the moles of Al (0.5 < 3.0), the limiting reagent is Fe2O3, and hence the molar ratio of Fe2O3 and Fe is used to find the moles of Fe. Fe2O3 : Fe 1 : 2 0.5 : x Where x = unknown number of moles of Fe By solving this by cross multiplication the number of moles of Fe come out to be 1.0 and therefore the answer is B.

questions.text: Four successive members of first row transition elements are listed below with their atomic numbers. Which one of them is expected to have the highest third ionization enthalpy:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Iron has the electron configuration [Ar] 4s² 3d⁶. Removing the third electron will leave it with [Ar] 4s¹ 3d⁵, which is not a particularly stable configuration, and does not require as much energy to achieve compared to manganese.

Chromium's configuration is [Ar] 4s¹ 3d⁵. Removing the third electron results in [Ar] 4s⁰ 3d⁴, which is less stable than its initial half-filled d-orbital configuration, but still not as energy-intensive as manganese's configuration change.

Manganese has the configuration [Ar] 4s² 3d⁵. Removing the third electron disrupts this stable half-filled d-orbital configuration, requiring significantly more energy, thus giving Mn the highest third ionization enthalpy.

Vanadium's configuration is [Ar] 4s² 3d³. Removing the third electron results in [Ar] 4s¹ 3d², which is more stable than the initial configuration, thus requiring less energy compared to manganese's situation.

questions.explanation: The third ionization enthalpy is the energy required to remove the third electron from a neutral atom. Manganese (Mn), with the configuration [Ar] 4s² 3d⁵, has a stable half-filled d-orbital. Disrupting this stable configuration requires a higher amount of energy compared to the other elements listed, as none of them are initially in a stable or half-filled state. Iron (Fe) and Chromium (Cr) do not lead to particularly stable configurations upon removal of the third electron, and Vanadium (V) becomes more stable upon losing the third electron. Therefore, Mn has the highest third ionization enthalpy among the given options.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct regarding the chirality (optical isomerism) of the cis and trans isomers of the type M(aa)2b2 (M stands for a metal, a and b are achiral ligands and aa is a bidentate ligand)?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The trans form is achiral and optically inactive while the cis form is chiral and exists in two enantiomeric forms
options.explanation: Cis is optically active and trans is optically inactive. For this question, you have to understand the fact that in the trans condition, the molecule has complete symmetry. The right and left halves are identical, alongside the top and bottom halves. This isn't the case for the cis molecule. The cis molecule only has the top and bottom halves bearing a resemblance; the left and right halves are different thus leading to the possibility of multiple enantiomers

options.text: Both the cis and trans forms are achiral and optically inactive
options.explanation: The trans form is achiral and optically inactive while the cis form is chiral and exists in two enantiomeric forms

options.text: The trans form is chiral and exists in two enantiomeric forms while the cis form is achiral and optically inactive.
options.explanation: The trans form is achiral and optically inactive while the cis form is chiral and exists in two enantiomeric forms

options.text: Both the cis and trans forms are chiral, and each exists in two enantiomeric forms.
options.explanation: The trans form is achiral and optically inactive while the cis form is chiral and exists in two enantiomeric forms

The right and left halves are identical, alongside the top and bottom halves. This isn't the case for the cis molecule. The cis molecule only has the top and bottom halves bearing a resemblance; the left and right halves are different thus leading to the possibility of multiple enantiomers

questions.text: A complex compound of cobalt has a molecular formula containing five NH3 molecules, one nitro group and two chlorine atoms for one Co atom. One mole of this compound produces three moles of ions in an aqueous solution. On reacting with excess AgNO3 solution, two moles of AgCl get precipitated. The ionic formula of the compound is?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The correct formula is [Co(NH3)5(NO2)] Cl2
The correct formula is [Co(NH3)5(NO2)] Cl2

As the compound gives 2 moles of AgCl on reacting with excess AgNO3, so there must be 2Cl present in the ionization sphere. So, the formula would be [Co(NH3)5(NO2)]Cl2. In addition: Cl2 isn't part of the complex rather it is an ion, attracted to the positive charge of the complex; This is why we don't consider option D.

As the compound gives 2 moles of AgCl on reacting with excess AgNO3, so there must be 2Cl present in the ionization sphere. So, the formula would be [Co(NH3)5(NO2)]Cl2. In addition: Cl2 isn't part of the complex rather it is an ion, attracted to the positive charge of the complex; This is why we don't consider option D.

In addition: Cl2 isn't part of the complex rather it is an ion, attracted to the positive charge of the complex; This is why we don't consider option D.

questions.text: If an excess of AgNO3 solution is added to 100 ml of a 0.024 M solution of dichlorobis(ethylene diamine) cobalt(III) chloride, how many moles of AgCl will be precipitated?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is incorrect. The calculation assumes an incorrect stoichiometry or molarity.
This is incorrect. The calculation doesn't account for the correct number of chloride ions per complex unit.
Correct. Each complex molecule provides one chloride ion, which reacts with AgNO3 to form AgCl. Calculate based on the given molarity and volume.
This is incorrect. It overestimates the number of chloride ions available for reaction.

questions.explanation: Since AgNO3 is in excess, the reaction is limited by the chloride ions present in the complex dichlorobis(ethylene diamine) cobalt(III) chloride. This complex provides one chloride ion per molecule. Given the solution concentration of 0.024 M and a volume of 100 ml (0.1 L), the number of moles of the complex, and hence chloride ions, is 0.024 M × 0.1 L = 0.0024 moles. Thus, 0.0024 moles of AgCl will be precipitated. Options A and B underestimate, while Option D overestimates the number of moles of AgCl formed.

questions.text: Which of the following complex will give a white precipitate with BaCl2 (aq)?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In this complex, SO4 is inside the coordination sphere. Therefore, it will not be available to react with BaCl2 to form BaSO4.
Here, SO4 is also within the coordination sphere, preventing it from reacting with BaCl2 to produce a white precipitate.
In this complex, SO4 is outside the coordination sphere, allowing it to react with BaCl2 to form a white precipitate of BaSO4.
This option is incorrect as only option C has SO4 outside the coordination sphere.

questions.explanation: A white precipitate with BaCl2 indicates the presence of free sulfate ions (SO42-) in the solution, which form insoluble BaSO4. Only the complex with the sulfate group in the outer ionization sphere. [Cr(NH3)5CI]SO4 will provide these free ions.

questions.text: The crystal field splitting energy for Cr+3 ion in an octahedral field increases for the ligands I–, H2O, NH3, CN– and the order is such that:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is the correct order because I– is the weakest field ligand, causing the least splitting, while CN– is the strongest, causing the most splitting.
This option is incorrect. CN– is the strongest field ligand and should be at the end of the series, not the beginning.
This option is incorrect. The positions of CN– and I– are reversed; CN– should be the strongest, not the weakest.
This option is incorrect. NH3 is a stronger field ligand than H2O, not weaker.

questions.explanation: In an octahedral field, the crystal field splitting energy depends on the strength of the ligands, as defined by the spectrochemical series. Weak field ligands, like I–, cause minimal splitting, while strong field ligands, like CN–, cause significant splitting. The correct order from weakest to strongest field ligand is: I– < H2O < NH3 < CN–. Incorrect options either reverse this order or misplace the positions of the ligands based on their field strength.

questions.text: Which of the following sets is/are example(s) of coordination isomerism complex?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Correct. These complexes demonstrate coordination isomerism due to interchange of ligands between the cations and anions.
Incorrect. These are hydrate isomers, as they differ in the number of water molecules and their positions.
Incorrect. These represent ionisation isomers, where the counter ions are swapped.
Incorrect. These represent polymerisation isomers, where the molecular formula is the same but the complexes differ in their degree of aggregation.

questions.explanation: Coordination isomerism occurs when there is an interchange of ligands between the cationic and anionic coordination spheres in a complex. In the given example, [Co(NH3)6] [Cr(CN)6] and [Co(CN)6] [Cr(NH3)6], the ammonia and cyanide ligands are exchanged between the cobalt and chromium coordination spheres, illustrating coordination isomerism.Option B involves a difference in the number of water molecules, which is characteristic of hydrate isomerism. Option C involves a swap of counter ions, indicative of ionisation isomerism. Option D involves changes in the degree of aggregation, characteristic of polymerisation isomerism.

questions.text: From the stability constants (hypothetical values), given below, predict which one is the strongest ligand?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ligand X has a stability constant of a certain value. Compare this with others to determine if it's the strongest. Remember, the highest stability constant indicates the strongest ligand.
Ligand Y has the highest stability constant among the options, indicating it is the strongest ligand as it forms the most stable complex.
Ligand Z has a lower stability constant than Ligand Y, making it a weaker ligand compared to Y in terms of stability.
Ligand W's stability constant is not the highest, indicating it is not the strongest ligand among the options as it forms less stable complexes.

In this case, Option B: Ligand Y has the highest stability constant, making it the strongest ligand. questions.text: A coordination complex compound of cobalt has a molecular formula containing five ammonia molecules, one nitro group, and two chlorine atoms for one cobalt atom. One mole of this compound produces three-mole ions in an aqueous solution. By the reaction of this solution with excess silver nitrate solution, two molecules of AgCl get precipitated. The ionic formula of this compound would be:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the complex does not match the stoichiometry provided in the question. It contains four ammonia molecules instead of five.
This option is incorrect as it suggests a structure where chloride ion is bonded to cobalt, which does not align with the given ionization behavior in solution.
This option is correct as it yields two moles of AgCl when reacted with AgNO3 and dissociates into three ions in solution, consistent with the problem statement.
This option is incorrect because it contains two nitro groups, which contradicts the molecular formula provided in the question.

questions.explanation: The correct ionic formula for the complex is [Co(NH3)5NO2]Cl2. This formula meets all the conditions described: it contains five ammonia molecules, one nitro group, and two chloride ions that dissociate in solution. The dissociation produces three ions: the cation [Co(NH3)5NO2]2+ and two Cl– ions. Upon reacting with AgNO3, the two Cl– ions form two moles of AgCl precipitate, confirming the structure. Other options do not fulfill these criteria due to incorrect stoichiometry or bonding arrangements.

questions.text: Each of the compounds Pt(NH3)6Cl4, Cr(NH3)6Cl3, Co(NH3)4Cl3 and K2Pt.Cl6 were dissolved in water to make its 0.001 M solution. The correct order of their increasing conductivity in solution is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Correct. K2PtCl6 dissociates into 3 ions, Co(NH3)4Cl3 into 4 ions, Cr(NH3)6Cl3 into 4 ions, and Pt(NH3)6Cl4 into 5 ions.
Incorrect. Cr(NH3)6Cl3 and Co(NH3)4Cl3 have the same number of ions but different complex structures affecting conductivity.
Incorrect. This order does not accurately reflect the number of ions each compound forms in solution.
Incorrect. Pt(NH3)6Cl4 dissociates into more ions than Co(NH3)4Cl3 and Cr(NH3)6Cl3, thus having higher conductivity.

questions.explanation: The conductivity of a solution is directly related to the number of ions the solute dissociates into. K2PtCl6 dissociates into 3 ions (K+, K+, PtCl62-), Co(NH3)4Cl3 into 4 ions (Co(NH3)43+, 3Cl-), Cr(NH3)6Cl3 into 4 ions (Cr(NH3)63+, 3Cl-), and Pt(NH3)6Cl4 into 5 ions (Pt(NH3)64+, 4Cl-). Therefore, the correct order of increasing conductivity is based on the number of ions: K2PtCl6 < Co(NH3)4Cl3 < Cr(NH3)6Cl3 < Pt(NH3)6Cl4.

questions.text: When FeCl3 reacts with K3[Fe(CN)6] no blue colour is observed. K3[Fe(CN)6] is used as an indicator in the estimation of Fe2+ by Cr2O7 2– ion in an acidic medium. End point will be when:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Solution of iron salt does not give blue colour with indicator.
options.explanation: When FeCl3 is present, Fe3+ ions oxidize Fe2+ ions, preventing the formation of the blue complex. At the endpoint, all Fe2+ is oxidized, so no blue color is observed.

options.text: Solution of iron salt gives blue colour with indicator.
options.explanation: This is incorrect because at the endpoint, all Fe2+ is oxidized to Fe3+, which does not form a blue complex with K3[Fe(CN)6].

options.text: No colour before and after with indicator.
options.explanation: Initially, when Fe2+ is present, a blue color can form. However, at the endpoint, the blue color disappears as Fe2+ is oxidized, not before.

options.text: Always blue colour before and after.
options.explanation: Incorrect, as the blue color only persists until all Fe2+ is oxidized. At the endpoint, no blue color remains.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A. When FeCl3 reacts with K3[Fe(CN)6, no blue color is observed because the Fe3+ ions in FeCl3 oxidize Fe2+ ions in K3[Fe(CN)6]. In the titration, K3[Fe(CN)6] acts as an indicator where the formation of a blue complex with Fe2+ indicates the presence of Fe2+. The endpoint is reached when all Fe2+ is oxidized to Fe3+, and the blue color disappears. Option B is incorrect as it assumes the presence of blue color with Fe3+. Option C is incorrect because it suggests no color change occurs, which is not true initially. Option D incorrectly implies a persistent blue color before and after the endpoint.

questions.text: Cr3+(aq) + 3H2O(l) —> Cr(H2O)63+ (aq)In the given reaction, the reactant H2O can be classified as a/ an
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Cr3+ is a Lewis acid because it accepts electrons, not donates, which is a characteristic of a Lewis base.
H2O is classified as a Lewis base because it donates a lone pair of electrons to Cr3+, which is the Lewis acid.
H2O cannot be classified as neutral in this context because it actively participates as a Lewis base by donating electrons.
While H2O can act as both an acid and a base in different reactions, in this specific reaction it acts as a Lewis base by donating electrons to Cr3+.

questions.explanation: In the reaction, Cr3+ is the Lewis acid because it accepts electron pairs, while H2O acts as the Lewis base by donating electron pairs to form the complex Cr(H2O)63+. Therefore, the correct classification for H2O in this context is a Lewis base. The other options are incorrect because they either misidentify the role of H2O or misinterpret its ability to donate electrons in this reaction.

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct name of [Co(en)2Cl2]Cl ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This name incorrectly describes the compound. It suggests three chlorine atoms and the wrong oxidation state for cobalt, which is actually +3. Furthermore, it doesn't properly account for the presence of ethylenediamine ligands.

options.text: Di chloro bis ethylene diamine cobalt (II) chloride
options.explanation: This option incorrectly identifies the oxidation state of cobalt as +2. The correct oxidation state is +3, given the formula of the complex.

options.text: Di ethylene diamine dichloro cobalt (II) chloride
options.explanation: This option incorrectly names the ligands and the oxidation state of cobalt. It should be 'bis ethylenediamine' and the oxidation state of cobalt should be +3, not +2.

options.text: Di chloro bis ethylene diamine cobalt (III) chloride
options.explanation: This is the correct name as it accurately reflects the ligands, their quantity, and the oxidation state of cobalt, which is +3.

questions.explanation: The correct name for the complex [Co(en)2Cl2]Cl is 'Di chloro bis ethylene diamine cobalt (III) chloride'. The prefix 'bis' is used for ethylenediamine because it's a polydentate ligand, and 'di chloro' indicates the presence of two chloride ions within the coordination sphere. The oxidation state of cobalt is +3, deduced from the charge balance: [Co(en)2Cl2]+ + Cl- gives an overall neutral complex. Other options fail to correctly identify the oxidation state, or the correct naming conventions for the ligands involved.

questions.text: Which of the following is most likely the structure of CrO3.6H2O if 1/3 of the total chlorine of the compound is precipitated by adding AgNO3 to its aqueous solution:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This formula means all three chloride ions are directly bonded to chromium. However, if 1/3 of total chlorine precipitates, there should be only one ionizable chloride, which isn't possible with this structure.

In this formula, all three chlorides are coordinated with chromium, suggesting there are no ionizable chlorides. However, 1/3 of the chlorine precipitates as AgCl, indicating one chloride must be ionizable, which this structure doesn't support.
This compound has one ionizable Cl- out of three, matching the condition that 1/3 of the total chlorine is precipitated as AgCl. Two Cl atoms are non-ionizable, being part of the coordination sphere.

This formula suggests one chloride is bonded to chromium, with two free chloride ions. This arrangement leads to two ionizable chlorides, not matching the condition that only 1/3 of chlorine is precipitated.

questions.explanation: The correct structure is [CrCl2(H2O)4]Cl.2H2O because it includes one ionizable chloride ion, which is consistent with the condition that 1/3 of the total chlorine is precipitated by AgNO3 as AgCl. In this formula, two chloride ions are part of the coordination sphere (non-ionizable), and one chloride is free (ionizable), which gets precipitated. Other options either have all chlorides non-ionizable or more than one ionizable chloride, which does not meet the given condition.

questions.text: Zinc does not show variable oxidation state, because
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: It is not true. Zinc's d-subshell is actually complete with 10 electrons.

Zinc (Zn) has a completely filled d-subshell (3d10) in its electronic configuration. This means it has achieved a stable electronic configuration, which makes it less likely to lose or gain electrons to form different oxidation states. As a result zinc primarily exhibits a +2 oxidation state in its compounds and does not show a wide range of variable oxidation states like some other transition metals.
This relates to its physical properties, not its variable oxidation state.

questions.text: Number of electrons involved in d-d transition of [Ti(H2O)6]+3
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is correct. In a d-d transition, one electron is excited from a lower energy d orbital to a higher energy d orbital within the same metal ion.
This is incorrect. A d-d transition typically involves only one electron moving between d orbitals.
This is incorrect. The transition involves a single electron within the d orbitals of the titanium ion.
This is incorrect. The d-d transition involves only one electron, not multiple electrons.

questions.explanation: The complex ion [Ti(H2O)6]+3 involves titanium in the +3 oxidation state. The electron configuration of Ti is [Ar] 4s2 3d2. When it forms a +3 ion, it loses three electrons, resulting in [Ar] 3d1. In a d-d transition, an electron from a lower energy d orbital is excited to a higher energy d orbital. Since only one electron is present in the 3d orbital, only one electron is involved in the transition. Thus, the correct answer is 1. Options 2, 3, and 4 are incorrect as they suggest more than one electron is involved in the transition, which is not the case.

Specifically, it produces four potassium ions (4K+) and one complex ion ([Fe(CN)6]4-). This dissociation results in the presence of both simple and complex ions in the solution.The other options are incorrect because they either consider only simple ions, only complex ions, or incorrectly suggest the formation of a weak acid, which does not occur in this specific dissociation process.

questions.text: When a mixture of white solids Z, is treated with an excess of dilute hydrochloric acid, a colourless gas is evolved and some, (but not all) of the mixture dissolve.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ba(NO₃)₂ does not react with HCl to produce a gas.Ca(OH)₂ reacts with HCl to form CaCl₂ and water, releasing no gas.Result: No colorless gas evolves.

BaSO₄ is insoluble in acids and does not react with HCl.CaCO₃ reacts with HCl to form CaCl₂, water, and CO₂, a colorless gas.Result: A colorless gas evolves, and some of the mixture dissolves, leaving BaSO₄ undissolved.

CaCO₃ reacts with HCl to produce CO₂, a colorless gas, and dissolves.MgSO₄ does not react with HCl to produce a gas and remains unchanged.Result: A colorless gas evolves, but not all of the mixture dissolves.

Ca(OH)₂ reacts with HCl to form CaCl₂ and water, with no gas evolution.MgCO₃ reacts with HCl to form MgCl₂, water, and CO₂, a colorless gas.Result: A colorless gas evolves, but not all of the mixture dissolves due to the presence of Ca(OH)₂.

questions.explanation: In the mixture of Ca(OH)2 and MgCO3, when treated with dilute HCl:Ca(OH)2 reacts to form CaCl2 and water, not producing any gas.MgCO3 reacts with HCl to form MgCl2, water, and CO2, which is a colorless gas.Thus, a colorless gas evolves, and not all of the mixture dissolves because Ca(OH)2 remains undissolved.Other options either do not produce a gas or result in complete dissolution of the mixture, which does not align with the question's conditions.

questions.text: The actinoids exhibit more oxidation states in general than the lanthanoids. This is because:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: The 5f orbitals are more buried than the 4f orbitals.
options.explanation: In both actinoids and lanthanoids, the 4f and 5f orbitals are actually quite similar in terms of their proximity to the nucleus. They are both considered to be "inner" orbitals, meaning they are shielded by the outer electron shells. Hence, this option is incorrect.

options.text: There is a similarity between 4f and 5f orbitals in their angular part of the wave function.
options.explanation: While both 4f and 5f orbitals have similar shapes in terms of their angular part of the wave function, the crucial difference lies in their radial part. The 4f orbitals are closer to the nucleus, while the 5f orbitals extend further out. Hence, this option is incorrect.

options.text: The actinoids are more reactive than the lanthanoids.
options.explanation: The reactivity of elements is influenced by various factors, such as atomic size, electron configuration, and electronegativity. While it is true that actinoids tend to exhibit a greater range of oxidation states compared to lanthanoids, it does not necessarily mean they are more reactive overall. Reactivity depends on specific elements and their interaction with other substances. Hence, this option is incorrect.

options.text: The 5f orbitals extend further from the nucleus than the 4f orbitals.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The 5f orbitals, being one energy level further from the nucleus than the 4f orbitals, have a larger principal quantum number. As a result, they have higher average energy and extend further from the nucleus, allowing for more interactions and a greater variety of oxidation states.

questions.explanation: The actinoids and lanthanoids are two series of elements in the periodic table. The actinoid series consists of elements with atomic numbers from 89 to 103, while the lanthanoid series includes elements with atomic numbers from 57 to 71. This statement is correct. The 5f orbitals, being one energy level further from the nucleus than the 4f orbitals, have a larger principal quantum number. As a result, they have higher average energy and extend further from the nucleus, allowing for more interactions and a greater variety of oxidation states.

questions.text: Bessemer's Converter is used in the manufacture of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The Bessemer process is not used to manufacture cast iron; instead, cast iron is typically produced directly from iron ore in blast furnaces.
Pig iron is the starting material for the Bessemer process, not the end product. It is converted into steel through this process.
Wrought iron is produced through different refining processes such as puddling, not through the Bessemer converter.
The Bessemer process is specifically designed for the mass production of steel by removing impurities from molten pig iron through oxidation.

questions.explanation: The Bessemer converter is an essential tool in the mass production of steel, as it efficiently removes impurities from molten pig iron using air blasts for oxidation. Unlike cast iron, pig iron, and wrought iron, which are either starting materials or produced through other methods, steel is the intended product of the Bessemer process, marking it as the correct answer. Cast iron is directly produced from iron ore, pig iron is transformed into steel, and wrought iron is made through puddling, not the Bessemer process.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is false?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Rusting of iron can be prevented by CO2
options.explanation: Carbon dioxide can contribute to rusting by forming carbonic acid in moisture, which accelerates corrosion. Thus, this statement is false.

options.text: Rusting of iron takes place in moist air
options.explanation: Moist air provides both oxygen and water, essential for the electrochemical process of rusting.

options.text: Rusting of iron is a chemical process
options.explanation: Rusting involves chemical reactions, primarily oxidation of iron, making it a chemical process.

options.text: Rusting of iron produces hydrated ferric oxide
options.explanation: The end product of rusting is hydrated iron(III) oxide, confirming this statement as true.

questions.explanation: The false statement is that rusting of iron can be prevented by CO2. In reality, CO2 can accelerate rusting by forming carbonic acid in the presence of moisture, which increases the rate of corrosion. Option B is true because rusting requires moist air. Option C is correct as rusting is indeed a chemical reaction. Option D is accurate as the rusting process results in the formation of hydrated ferric oxide.

questions.text: The most durable metal plating on iron to protect against corrosion is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Tin is less reactive than iron, so it can protect iron as long as the coating is intact. However, if the tin coating is scratched, iron will corrode faster because iron acts as an anode to tin in the electrochemical cell formed.

Zinc is more electropositive than iron, meaning it is more easily oxidized. In a galvanic cell formed by a scratch in the zinc coating, zinc will corrode preferentially, protecting the iron. This is known as galvanization and provides excellent long-term corrosion protection.
Copper is less reactive than iron. If the copper coating is scratched, iron will corrode preferentially and at an accelerated rate.
Nickel is also less reactive than iron. Similar to tin and copper plating, if the nickel coating is damaged, the underlying iron will corrode.

questions.explanation: Zinc is more electropositive than iron, meaning it is more easily oxidized. In a galvanic cell formed by a scratch in the zinc coating, zinc will corrode preferentially, protecting the iron. This is known as galvanization and provides excellent long-term corrosion protection.

questions.text: The colour of Fe(2+) ion is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Blue color is typically associated with Cu(2+) ions rather than Fe(2+). Therefore, this is not the correct color for Fe(2+) ions.
This is the correct answer. Aqueous solutions of iron(II) salts (Fe(2+)) typically exhibit a pale green or light green color due to the electronic transitions within the d-orbitals of the iron ion.
Yellow color is more characteristic of iron(III) ions (Fe(3+)) in aqueous solutions, often due to hydrolysis forming colored hydroxo complexes. Hence, this is not the correct color for Fe(2+).
Brown color is often observed in concentrated solutions of Fe(3+) or in the presence of rust, which is hydrated iron(III) oxide. Fe(2+) ions do not typically exhibit a brown color.

questions.explanation: The light green color of Fe(2+) ions in aqueous solutions is due to the specific electronic transitions within the d-orbitals of the iron ion. This is a common characteristic of many iron(II) compounds. The other colors mentioned are associated with different ions or conditions: blue is linked with Cu(2+) ions, yellow is typical for Fe(3+) ions due to hydrolysis, and brown occurs in concentrated Fe(3+) solutions or rust, not in Fe(2+).

questions.text: Oxidation number of Fe in K3Fe(CN)6 is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: If the oxidation state of Fe were +1, the total charge of [Fe(CN)6] would be -5, which does not match the neutral compound requirement.
An oxidation state of +2 for Fe would make the complex ion charge -4, leading to K4Fe(CN)6, which does not match the compound's formula.
Fe has an oxidation state of +3. This results in the complex ion [Fe(CN)6] having a charge of -3, which balances with 3 K+ ions, making the compound neutral.
If the oxidation state of Fe were +4, the charge of [Fe(CN)6] would be -2, leading to K2Fe(CN)6, which is not the correct formula.

questions.explanation: The oxidation state of iron (Fe) in K3Fe(CN)6 can be determined by considering the oxidation states of the other components. Potassium (K) has an oxidation state of +1, and cyanide (CN) has an oxidation state of -1. The compound is neutral, so the sum of oxidation states must equal zero. Thus, 3(+1) + x + 6(-1) = 0, where x is the oxidation state of Fe. Simplifying gives x = +3. This means the correct oxidation state of Fe is +3.Options A, B, and D are incorrect because they lead to charges on the complex ion that do not match the given neutral compound formula K3Fe(CN)6. Only option C maintains the neutrality and matches the correct formula.

questions.text: Mohr's salt is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is the correct chemical formula for Mohr's salt, which is ammonium iron(II) sulfate hexahydrate. It is a double salt containing both ferrous sulfate and ammonium sulfate.
This is the formula for potash alum, a double salt consisting of aluminum sulfate and potassium sulfate, not related to Mohr's salt.
This is the formula for green vitriol or ferrous sulfate heptahydrate, which is a single salt and not a double salt like Mohr's salt.
This is the formula for blue vitriol or copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate, which is unrelated to Mohr's salt.

questions.explanation: Mohr's salt is correctly represented by the formula FeSO4.(NH4)2SO4.6H2O. It is a double salt composed of ferrous sulfate and ammonium sulfate, crystallized with six molecules of water. This distinguishes it from other salts such as potash alum (Al2(SO4)3.K2SO4.24H2O), which contains aluminum and potassium instead of iron and ammonium. Similarly, green vitriol (FeSO4.7H2O) and blue vitriol (CuSO4.5H2O) are single salts, lacking the combination of two different sulfate salts that characterize double salts like Mohr's salt.

questions.text: Blue Vitriol is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Cobalt(II) chloride is typically pink when hydrated and blue when anhydrous, but it is not referred to as blue vitriol.
This is the chemical formula for blue vitriol, also known as copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate, which is a bright blue crystalline solid.
This is the chemical formula for green vitriol or ferrous sulfate heptahydrate, which is a light green crystalline solid. It is not blue vitriol.
Zinc sulfate, commonly in the form ZnSO4.7H2O, is known as white vitriol and is a colorless crystalline solid. It is not blue vitriol.

questions.explanation: Blue vitriol is the common name for copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4.5H2O), which is characterized by its bright blue color. Option A (CoCl2) is incorrect because cobalt(II) chloride is blue only when anhydrous and not known as blue vitriol. Option C (FeSO4.7H2O) represents green vitriol, which is green, not blue. Option D (ZnSO4) is typically referred to as white vitriol and is colorless, not blue. Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.

questions.text: IUPAC name of [Ni(CO)4] is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is a common name for the compound, not an IUPAC name. It does not specify the number of ligands or the oxidation state of nickel.
While this name indicates the presence of four carbonyl ligands, it is incomplete because it lacks the oxidation state of nickel.
This is the correct IUPAC name. It specifies the number of carbonyl ligands (four), the central nickel atom, and its oxidation state (0).
This is incorrect because "Tetra Carbonyl Nickel(0)" is the correct IUPAC name.

questions.explanation: The correct IUPAC name for [Ni(CO)4] is "Tetra Carbonyl Nickel(0)." This name correctly describes the compound by indicating the number of carbonyl ligands (four) and the central metal atom (nickel), along with its oxidation state (0). The oxidation state is essential in IUPAC nomenclature, and it is shown in parentheses. Options A and B fail to provide complete information, while Option D is incorrect because Option C is the correct name.

questions.text: One of the most important ways of protection of a metal from corrosion is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alloying involves mixing a metal with other metals or nonmetals to improve its properties, including corrosion resistance (e.g., stainless steel).

Electroplating involves coating a metal surface with a thin layer of another metal using electrolysis. This can protect the underlying metal from corrosion if the coating metal is less reactive or provides a barrier.

Galvanizing is a specific type of electroplating or hot-dipping process that coats iron or steel with a layer of zinc. Zinc is more electropositive than iron, so even if the coating is scratched, zinc will corrode preferentially (sacrificial protection), protecting the iron. This makes galvanizing a very effective method for corrosion prevention.

Applying paints, plastics, or other non-metallic coatings can create a barrier that prevents moisture and air from reaching the metal surface, thus preventing corrosion.While all listed methods offer corrosion protection, galvanizing is often considered one of the most important and effective for iron and steel due to the sacrificial protection offered by zinc.

questions.explanation: Galvanizing is a specific type of electroplating or hot-dipping process that coats iron or steel with a layer of zinc. Zinc is more electropositive than iron, so even if the coating is scratched, zinc will corrode preferentially (sacrificial protection), protecting the iron. This makes galvanizing a very effective method for corrosion prevention.

questions.text: Copper sulphate is commonly known as:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Green vitriol is the common name for iron(II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4.7H2O), which is not related to copper sulfate.
White vitriol refers to zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4.7H2O), not copper sulfate.
Blue vitriol is the common name for copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4.5H2O), known for its bright blue color.
This option is incorrect as copper sulfate is indeed known as blue vitriol.

questions.explanation: Copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4.5H2O) is commonly known as Blue Vitriol due to its striking blue color. This distinguishes it from other vitriols, such as Green Vitriol (iron(II) sulfate) and White Vitriol (zinc sulfate), which are not associated with copper. The option 'None of the above' is incorrect because Blue Vitriol is indeed the common name for copper sulfate.

questions.text: Photographic films and plates have an essential coating of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Silver nitrate is an important precursor in photography, but it is not the direct light-sensitive agent in photographic films and plates. It is used to react with halides to produce silver halides, which are the sensitive components.
Sodium bromide provides bromide ions to form silver bromide, but it is not itself the light-sensitive compound in photographic emulsions.
Silver bromide is the main light-sensitive compound used in photographic films and plates. It reacts to light exposure by forming a latent image, which is developed to produce visible images.
This option is incorrect because silver bromide is indeed the essential light-sensitive component in photographic films and plates.

questions.explanation: Silver bromide (AgBr) is the correct answer because it serves as the primary light-sensitive compound in photographic emulsions. Upon exposure to light, silver bromide undergoes a photochemical reaction to create a latent image, which is then developed to form a visible photograph. Silver nitrate (AgNO3) is not directly light-sensitive but is used to create silver halides like silver bromide through chemical reactions with halide ions. Sodium bromide (NaBr) provides bromide ions for this reaction but is not itself light-sensitive. The 'None of the above' option is incorrect because silver bromide is indeed the essential component.

questions.text: Chromium compounds in which oxidation state is 6+ behaves as:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Chromium compounds with a +6 oxidation state are not typically square planar. This geometry is more common for certain d8 metal ions.
Chromium in a +6 oxidation state can form tetrahedral complexes, especially in oxoanions like chromate (CrO42−).
Trigonal bipyramidal geometry is not typical for chromium in the +6 oxidation state.
Chromium in a +6 oxidation state does not typically form octahedral complexes. Octahedral geometries are more common in lower oxidation states.

questions.explanation: Chromium in the +6 oxidation state often forms tetrahedral complexes, such as in chromate ions (CrO42−) and dichromate ions (Cr2O72−). These are typical examples where the tetrahedral geometry is favored. Square planar and trigonal bipyramidal geometries are not common for this oxidation state of chromium. Octahedral geometry is generally associated with lower oxidation states, such as Cr(III).

questions.text: Choose the compound tetraamine aqua Chlorocobalt(III)chloride:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option correctly reflects the coordination compound tetraamine aqua chlorocobalt(III) chloride, with the right number of ligands and charges.
This option incorrectly has more water molecules and fewer ammonia molecules than required by 'tetraamine'.
This option has an incorrect number of ammonia ligands and lacks the aqua ligand, which is essential in the compound.
This option incorrectly includes too many chloride ions within the coordination sphere and lacks the aqua ligand.

questions.explanation: The correct formula for tetraamine aqua chlorocobalt(III) chloride is [Co(NH3)4(H2O)Cl]Cl2. The name indicates four ammonia ligands (tetraamine) and one water molecule (aqua) coordinated to the cobalt center. Additionally, one chloride ion is part of the coordination sphere, and two chloride ions are outside the coordination sphere to balance the charge. Other options either have the wrong number of ligands or incorrect ligands.

questions.text: Which is good quality iron are containing low phosphorus content?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Hematite is a common iron ore and has a lower phosphorus content compared to some other ores, but it is not the best in terms of purity.
Limonite is an iron ore with higher impurities and typically has a higher phosphorus content compared to magnetite.
Siderite often contains significant amounts of phosphorus and is not considered a high-quality source of iron.
Magnetite is known for its high iron content and low phosphorus impurities, making it a high-quality source of iron.

questions.explanation: Magnetite is considered a high-quality iron ore due to its high iron content and low levels of impurities such as phosphorus. Phosphorus can make iron brittle, so low phosphorus content is desirable. Hematite also contains low phosphorus levels but not as low as magnetite. Limonite and siderite generally have higher phosphorus content, making them less desirable for high-quality iron production.

questions.text: Which one is considered as fool'sgold?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Copper metal is reddish-brown and not known as fool's gold.
Iron pyrite, also known as pyrite or fool's gold, is often mistaken for gold because of its metallic luster and pale brass-yellow color. It is the correct answer.
Copper glance is a mineral of copper but does not resemble gold in appearance.
This option is incorrect as there is indeed a mineral referred to as fool's gold.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Iron pyrite (FeS2). It is often referred to as fool's gold because its color and metallic luster make it resemble gold. However, unlike gold, it is brittle and will break rather than bend. Copper metal and copper glance do not resemble gold in appearance, and they are not referred to as fool's gold.

questions.text: Which of the following furnaces is Bused for the production of wroughtiron?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This furnace is primarily used for making steel, not wrought iron.
This type of furnace is used for the production of wrought iron by refining the iron and allowing impurities to be removed.
The Bessemer converter is used for converting pig iron into steel, not for making wrought iron.
A blast furnace is used to extract iron from its ore, but it does not produce wrought iron directly.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the Reverberatory furnace. This type of furnace is used for refining pig iron into wrought iron by exposing the iron to a high temperature and allowing the impurities to be oxidized and removed. In contrast, an Open hearth furnace and a Bessemer converter are primarily used for steel production. The Blast furnace is used to extract iron from its ore, but it does not produce wrought iron directly. Instead, it produces pig iron, which then needs further refining in a reverberatory furnace to become wrought iron.

questions.text: Which one of the following is not a physical property;
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Corrosion is a chemical property because it involves a chemical reaction that changes the material's composition.
Solubility is a physical property as it describes how a substance dissolves in a solvent without changing its chemical composition.
Melting point is a physical property because it is the temperature at which a solid becomes a liquid without a change in chemical composition.
Boiling point is a physical property as it signifies the temperature at which a liquid turns into vapor while maintaining its chemical identity.

questions.explanation: Corrosion is not a physical property; it is a chemical property because it involves a chemical transformation of the material, often involving oxidation. This process fundamentally changes the substance's composition, unlike physical properties which involve changes in state or appearance without altering the chemical makeup. Solubility, melting point, and boiling point are all physical properties as they describe changes in state or solubility without a change in chemical identity.

questions.text: The oxidation number of cobalt in[Co(EN),H2O(CN)]2+
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The oxidation state of cobalt is not +2. Consider the neutral charges of ethylenediamine and water, and the -1 charge of cyanide.
Correct! The oxidation state of cobalt is +3, which accounts for the neutral ligands and the -1 charge of cyanide, balancing the +2 charge of the complex.
The oxidation state of cobalt cannot be +4 due to the combination of the neutral ligands and the charge of cyanide.
+5 is not a possible oxidation state for cobalt in this complex, given the charges of the ligands and the overall charge.

questions.explanation: To determine the oxidation number of cobalt in the complex [Co(en),H2O(CN)]2+, consider the charges: ethylenediamine (en) and water (H2O) are neutral, while cyanide (CN) has a charge of -1. The overall charge of the complex is +2. Therefore, the equation is: OS(Co) + (0) + (0) + (-1) = +2. Solving for the oxidation state of cobalt, we find it to be +3. Thus, Option B is correct. Options A, C, and D are incorrect as they do not correctly balance the overall charge of the complex.

questions.text: Ammonium hydroxide was addedto a salt solution deep blue colorwas obtaine. The solution containsions of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Zinc (Zn2+) ions do not form a deep blue colored complex with ammonium hydroxide.
Copper (Cu2+) ions form a deep blue colored complex called tetraamminecopper(II) when reacted with ammonium hydroxide.
Iron (Fe3+) ions do not form a deep blue complex with ammonium hydroxide; they usually form brown or yellow complexes.
Barium (Ba2+) ions do not form colored complexes with ammonium hydroxide.

questions.explanation: The deep blue color observed upon the addition of ammonium hydroxide indicates the formation of a complex with copper(II) ions. This is due to the formation of the tetraamminecopper(II) complex, [Cu(NH3)4(H2O)2]2+, which is characteristically deep blue. Zinc, iron, and barium ions do not produce such a color change with ammonium hydroxide.

questions.text: The oxidation number of iron in(Fe(CN)6)4- is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This would imply a different charge balance in the complex than is observed.
Correct! The complex overall is neutral, and each cyanide ion carries a -1 charge.
This oxidation state would lead to an incorrect net charge for the complex.
This oxidation state does not match the charge balance required for the complex.

questions.explanation: In the complex Fe(CN)64-, iron is surrounded by six cyanide ions, each with a charge of -1. The total charge contributed by the cyanide ions is -6. Since the overall charge of the complex is -4, the oxidation state of iron must be +2 to balance the charges (i.e., +2 - 6 = -4). This makes option B: +2 the correct answer. Option A: +3, Option C: +4, and Option D: +6 do not satisfy the charge balance required for the complex.

questions.text: Complexes with bidentate ligandsare called;
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While ligands are molecules or ions that donate pairs of electrons to a central metal atom, this term is not specific to bidentate ligands.
Chelates are complexes formed when bidentate or polydentate ligands bind to a metal ion, creating a ring-like structure.
Complexes refer to any combination of a central metal atom or ion and ligands, not specifically those with bidentate ligands.
This is not correct as 'Chelates' is indeed the term used for complexes with bidentate ligands.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Chelates. In coordination chemistry, chelates are complexes where ligands contain two or more donor atoms that bind to a single metal ion, forming ring-like structures. Bidentate ligands have two donor atoms that allow them to form such chelate rings. Option A, 'Ligands,' is incorrect as it is a general term for electron-pair donating species, not specific to bidentate ligands. Option C, 'Complexes,' is incorrect because it is a broad term for any coordination entity, not specifically those involving bidentate ligands. Option D, 'None of the above,' is incorrect since 'Chelates' is the precise term for the described complex type.

questions.text: Which of the following is nottransition element;
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Zinc (Zn) has a full d10 electron configuration and does not have partially filled d-orbitals.
Chromium (Cr) is a transition element because it has partially filled d-orbitals with the electron configuration [Ar] 3d5 4s1.
Manganese (Mn) is a transition element with the electron configuration [Ar] 3d5 4s2, indicating partially filled d-orbitals.
Nickel (Ni) is a transition element with the electron configuration [Ar] 3d8 4s2, which includes partially filled d-orbitals.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Zinc (Zn). Transition elements are defined as elements that have partially filled d-orbitals. Zinc, with an electron configuration of [Ar] 3d10 4s2, has a completely filled d-subshell, and thus does not meet this criterion. In contrast, Chromium (Cr), Manganese (Mn), and Nickel (Ni) all have partially filled d-orbitals, qualifying them as transition elements.

questions.text: [NiCl4]2- is tetrahedral shaped complex, the bond angle between <CI - Ni - C|> is;
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 120° is the bond angle for a trigonal planar geometry, not tetrahedral.
107° is close to the bond angle for a tetrahedral geometry with lone pair repulsion, but not the ideal angle.
105° is the bond angle for water, which is bent due to two lone pairs, not applicable to a tetrahedral complex.
109.5° is the ideal bond angle for a perfect tetrahedral geometry.

questions.explanation: The complex [NiCl4]2- is a tetrahedral complex, which means it adopts a geometry where the bond angle between any two bonds is approximately 109.5°. This is because the four bonds are equally spaced around the central atom, minimizing repulsion according to VSEPR theory. Option A (120°) is incorrect because it pertains to a trigonal planar geometry. Option B (107°) is typical for a distorted tetrahedral geometry due to lone pair interactions, not applicable here.
